problem: Existing few-shot benchmarks for rare word embeddings (DN, CRW, Chimera) let hybrid/form-based models exploit morphological overlap with test items, obscuring true context-learning performance.
method: Analyze stem overlap in DN/CRW and show a simple stem-averaging baseline can dominate; introduce three revised evaluations—Filtered DN and Filtered CRW (remove same-stem words from background embeddings) and Full Chimera (expanded set)—to fairly compare context-based vs hybrid methods.
paper_title: Bad Form: Comparing Context-Based and Form-Based Few-Shot Learning in Distributional Semantic Models

problem: Continuing Word2Vec/FastText training for a new word with few contexts distorts background embeddings and prior work under-optimizes hyperparameters, hurting few-shot quality.
method: Selective Word2Vec/FastText that update only the target (test) word and exclude it from negative sampling, combined with targeted hyperparameter tuning (e.g., higher LR, larger windows/epochs for DN), yielding consistent gains over standard baselines.
paper_title: Bad Form: Comparing Context-Based and Form-Based Few-Shot Learning in Distributional Semantic Models

problem: Addition-based few-shot estimators (Additive, A La Carte, FCM) underweight informative contexts and ALC’s linear transform limits expressiveness.
method: Inject Word2Vec-inspired weighting into addition (distance-based window weights, subsampling of frequent words, negative-sample correction) and replace ALC’s linear map with a 1-hidden-layer neural projection; integrate this into a Neural Form-Context Model with gated form/context weighting, achieving new state of the art on 4/6 tasks.
paper_title: Bad Form: Comparing Context-Based and Form-Based Few-Shot Learning in Distributional Semantic Models

problem: In LMaaS settings, users only have inference API access to large PTMs (no weights or gradients), making it hard to exploit labeled data for task-specific prompt optimization.
method: Black-Box Tuning (BBT): optimize continuous (soft) prefix prompts via derivative-free optimization (CMA-ES), iteratively querying the model API to minimize a supervised loss; enables client-side prompt learning without gradient access.
paper_title: Black-Box Tuning for Language-Model-as-a-Service

problem: Derivative-free optimization is intractable in the high-dimensional soft-prompt space (tens of thousands of parameters).
method: Random-embedding subspace optimization: search over a low-dimensional vector z and map it to prompt increments via a fixed random linear projection A (entries sampled from a uniform He-style initializer), added to an initial prompt p0; bounded-box search leverages PTMs’ low intrinsic dimensionality to make black-box optimization feasible.
paper_title: Black-Box Tuning for Language-Model-as-a-Service

problem: Sparse reward signals and poor initialization hinder stable, data-efficient black-box prompt learning in few-shot regimes.
method: Use informative losses (cross-entropy or hinge) instead of negative accuracy, and pre-train a reusable prompt embedding on MNLI for sentence-pair tasks to initialize p0; these choices stabilize DFO-based prompt tuning and yield strong few-shot performance surpassing manual prompts, in-context learning, and gradient-based prompt/full-model tuning.
paper_title: Black-Box Tuning for Language-Model-as-a-Service

problem: Indonesian NLP resources are scattered, inconsistently formatted, and often not publicly accessible, hindering progress across 19+ local languages.
method: Introduce NusaCrowd, a two-part open framework—NusaCatalogue (metadata) and NusaCrowd Data Hub (standardized loaders)—that unifies 137 datasets via 13 task schemas and 118 dataloaders, performs manual/automatic quality checks (incl. LID and annotation-process audits), and opens access to 14 previously private datasets.
paper_title: NusaCrowd: Open Source Initiative for Indonesian NLP Resources

problem: Erasure-based NLP attributions replace tokens with fixed values (e.g., zero), creating out-of-distribution inputs that inflate/deflate importance scores and yield misleading explanations.
method: Input marginalization: compute the counterfactual p(y|x−i) by summing model outputs over substitute tokens weighted by their contextual likelihoods from a BERT masked language model; score contributions via weight of evidence and extend to multi-token marginalization for joint effects.
paper_title: Interpretation of NLP models through input marginalization

problem: There is no zero-shot benchmark to assess cross-lingual generalization of NLU/NLG for Indonesian and regional languages.
method: Build the first Indonesian multilingual zero-shot benchmarks—NusaNLU (26 datasets, 12 languages) and NusaNLG (36 datasets, MT+summarization)—and evaluate BLOOMZ, XGLM, and XLM-R with prompting and cross-task NLI transfer; findings show instruction-tuned BLOOMZ > XGLM, and small XLM-R with NLI intermediate training can rival prompting, while local-language NLG remains weak.
paper_title: NusaCrowd: Open Source Initiative for Indonesian NLP Resources

problem: Exact marginalization over the full vocabulary (>30k tokens) is computationally prohibitive for token-level attribution.
method: Adaptively truncated marginalization: include only candidate substitutions whose MLM likelihood exceeds a threshold σ and renormalize the weights, achieving large speedups while maintaining near-identical attributions to full marginalization.
paper_title: Interpretation of NLP models through input marginalization

problem: Common deletion-based evaluation metrics reintroduce OOD artifacts by zeroing/removing tokens, undermining faithfulness assessment.
method: AUCrep metric: progressively replace high-attribution tokens with samples drawn from the BERT MLM distribution (up to 20% due to MLM training regime) and compute area under the deletion curve, enabling fair comparison and showing improved faithfulness over zero/[UNK] erasure.
paper_title: Interpretation of NLP models through input marginalization

problem: Deployed NLP models must be continually updated from their own mistakes under boundary-agnostic, non-stationary OOD streams, but existing continual learning setups are task-incremental and lack suitable online metrics.
method: Define Continual Model Refinement (CMR), an error-centric continual learning formulation for a fixed task, and introduce five evaluation metrics (instant error-fixing EFR, upstream/online knowledge retention UKR/OKR, cumulative success CSR, and held-out generalization KG) plus an overall criterion for concise benchmarking.
paper_title: On Continual Model Refinement in Out-of-Distribution Data Streams

problem: There is no realistic, controllable way to generate OOD data streams that capture non-stationarity, mixture of multiple OOD clusters per episode, and the decay of in-distribution traffic.
method: Propose a general sampling algorithm that builds query streams by mixing (i) an in-distribution cluster with exponentially decaying weight (alpha), (ii) a major OOD cluster that evolves via a Markov chain (beta), and (iii) diverse residual OOD clusters (gamma), enabling validation/test streams with tunable dynamics.
paper_title: On Continual Model Refinement in Out-of-Distribution Data Streams

problem: Refining models on sparse error streams risks catastrophic forgetting; off-the-shelf CL methods are not tailored to error-centric NLP with large upstream data.
method: Adapt CL to CMR with replay-centric designs: a bi-memory scheme (separate upstream memory Mu=D and growing online error memory Mo) and mixed-tuning that jointly trains on retrieved memory Rt and current errors Et; introduce MaxLoss conditional replay that ranks candidates by virtual loss under a model updated on Et (more stable than loss-delta MIR), and show combining replay with Online L2 regularization further boosts overall performance and OOD generalization.
paper_title: On Continual Model Refinement in Out-of-Distribution Data Streams

problem: Chemists lack open, high-recall access to fine-grained reaction details (e.g., concentrations, reaction times, order of addition) because existing open resources are coarse and proprietary databases are costly.
method: Construct an open knowledge base by automatically extracting sentence-level semantic action graphs and protocol-level slots from 6M patent procedures using ProcBERT-based Transformer IE fine-tuned on CHEMSYN (mention identification and argument role labeling) and ChEMU (slot filling), yielding richly structured, scalable representations for search.
paper_title: SYNKB: Semantic Search for Synthetic Procedures

problem: Existing databases cannot answer operation-level questions that require reasoning over relations between entities (e.g., which reagent was used to dilute plasma, or order-specific measurements).
method: Introduce a semantic graph search over extracted action graphs using an Odinson-based graph query language with typed nodes/edges and named captures, integrated with fast Lucene/Elasticsearch indexing; combined with slot-based filters, this enables expressive, operation-aware queries and achieves higher recall than Reaxys/USPTO-Lowe while maintaining high precision.
paper_title: SYNKB: Semantic Search for Synthetic Procedures

problem: There is no realistic benchmark to study vision foundation models in truly low-resource scenarios that combine extreme data scarcity, fine-grained distinctions, and strong domain shift from natural images.
method: Create the LITE benchmark with three specialized tasks—circuit diagram classification, historic map retrieval, and mechanical drawing retrieval—using genuinely scarce data and standardized splits/metrics to reveal failure modes of current foundation models and transfer methods.
paper_title: Low-Resource Vision Challenges for Foundation Models

problem: Severe data scarcity causes overfitting, and prior generative augmentation mostly yields label-preserving samples, limiting diversity.
method: Diffusion-based augmentation with Stable Diffusion that generates both label-preserving images (low-noise) used in supervised task loss and label-breaking images (higher-noise) used with a contrastive objective via paired augmentations and a memory bank, enlarging the data space without labels.
paper_title: Low-Resource Vision Challenges for Foundation Models

problem: Transformer attention in foundation models is ill-suited to specialized low-resource domains, failing to focus on task-relevant spatial patterns.
method: Learn a small set of global, domain-specific attention maps shared across images/tokens; crop token-aligned submaps and fuse their outputs with standard multi-head self-attention via learned weights, adding minimal parameters to adapt attention patterns (e.g., vertical/horizontal structures, local neighborhoods).
paper_title: Low-Resource Vision Challenges for Foundation Models

problem: Pharmacovigilance requires extracting structured adverse drug event (ADE) data from full biomedical articles, but existing ADE resources are small, span-annotated, and not document-level, limiting scalability and real-world utility.
method: Construct BioDEX by automatically linking FDA FAERS Individual Case Safety Reports to their originating PubMed/PMC papers via high-precision title matching, yielding 65k abstracts, 19k full texts, and 256k expert reports. Define a document-level Report-Extraction task (seriousness, patient sex, drugs, reactions), introduce a strict weighted F1 metric, and estimate human agreement (72% F1).
paper_title: BioDEX: Large-Scale Biomedical Adverse Drug Event Extraction for Real-World Pharmacovigilance

problem: LLMs struggle to extract long-context, structured ADE information from full-text biomedical articles using few-shot prompting.
method: Formulate report extraction as sequence-to-sequence generation and fine-tune FLAN-T5 (Large) with 2048-token inputs and stringified outputs for the four core fields, achieving 62.3% F1 (val) and 59.1% (test) and outperforming GPT-3.5/4 few-shot baselines (≤53.1% F1), especially on drug and reaction sets.
paper_title: BioDEX: Large-Scale Biomedical Adverse Drug Event Extraction for Real-World Pharmacovigilance

problem: Literature triage for specific drugs/reactions relies on keyword lookups with high recall but poor precision, wasting reviewer effort.
method: Leverage BioDEX-trained model predictions to classify the presence of specific reactions/drugs per paper, improving macro-F1 over lookup baselines and enabling higher-precision prioritization for pharmacovigilance reviewers.
paper_title: BioDEX: Large-Scale Biomedical Adverse Drug Event Extraction for Real-World Pharmacovigilance

problem: Safety-aligned LLMs are fragile and can be jailbroken; training-time methods struggle to remain safe across diverse contexts without hurting utility.
method: Safety Amplification (SA): derive a safety amplification vector from a few unsafe-question/safe-answer demonstrations by identifying influential attention heads via activation patching and averaging safety-conditioned activations; inject this vector into a selected intermediate layer during the forward pass to steer the latent state toward safer behavior in-context.
paper_title: SAFEINFER: Context Adaptive Decoding Time Safety Alignment for Large Language Models

problem: Even with safer activations, decoding can still pick harmful tokens; need to suppress harmful tendencies without degrading model capabilities.
method: Safety-Guided Decoding (sGDS): train a small "unsafe" anti-expert model on harmful Q/A, then combine its distribution with the (amplified) target model using the Union operator from language-model arithmetic and apply a token-level constraint with a lambda weight to downweight harmful subsets, producing a safety-optimized next-token distribution while preserving utility.
paper_title: SAFEINFER: Context Adaptive Decoding Time Safety Alignment for Large Language Models

problem: Lack of a comprehensive, policy-grounded benchmark to evaluate LLM harmfulness across categories and attack settings.
method: HARMEVAL: a new benchmark (~550 prompts) spanning 11 prohibited-use categories (aligned with OpenAI/Meta policies), curated via GPT-4 harm labeling and Perspective API toxicity filtering, enabling thorough evaluation under simple, instruction-centric, CoT, and jailbreak prompts.
paper_title: SAFEINFER: Context Adaptive Decoding Time Safety Alignment for Large Language Models

problem: Traditional task-oriented, turn-level DST fails in open-domain LLM chats where conversations span multiple intents, topics, and long contexts, making intent tracking ambiguous and inconsistent.
method: Redefine DST as a joint task: predict topic-cohesive segment boundaries and perform per-segment state tracking (slots/values) so states can diverge across segments. Formalize segment-level states and jointly infer boundaries and states within a single framework.
paper_title: S3-DST: Structured Open-Domain Dialogue Segmentation and State Tracking in the Era of LLMs

problem: Zero-shot DST in open-domain settings needs reliable, parseable outputs and consistency within segments; unstructured prompts cause drift and hallucinations.
method: S3-DST’s structured prompting: use hierarchical XML for both inputs (turn-indexed user/agent XML) and outputs (turn-level tags for boundary, intent, domain) with explicit valid label lists and a rule enforcing identical labels within a segment. This constrains generation, eases parsing, and improves zero-shot robustness.
paper_title: S3-DST: Structured Open-Domain Dialogue Segmentation and State Tracking in the Era of LLMs

problem: LLMs struggle to maintain and ground long-context information, leading to forgetting and hallucination, especially as dialogue length grows.
method: Pre-Analytical Recollection (PAR): require a concise (≤3 sentences) per-turn summary before predicting segmentation and state tags, explicitly grounding each prediction in the turn’s content. This improves long-context tracking and reduces hallucinations, yielding SOTA zero-shot results on Bing Chat, MultiWOZ 2.1/2.4, and DialSeg711.
paper_title: S3-DST: Structured Open-Domain Dialogue Segmentation and State Tracking in the Era of LLMs

problem: LLM-generated research ideas are often ungrounded, hallucinated, and lack traceable motivational rationale.
method: Introduce MotivGraph, a motivational knowledge graph that encodes (problem, challenge, solution) triples with hierarchical parent-of links. An agent queries the graph via fuzzy search and relation retrieval (plus Semantic Scholar) at each ideation step and must reference specific nodes/sources, providing structured grounding and traceability for every idea component.
paper_title: MotivGraph-SoIQ: Integrating Motivational Knowledge Graphs and Socratic Dialogue for Enhanced LLM Ideation

problem: LLMs exhibit confirmation bias and weak self-correction, leading to superficial or unfalsified idea refinements.
method: Develop a Q-Driven Socratic Ideator: a dual-agent loop where a mentor-only questioner challenges innovation, feasibility, and rationality while a researcher refines ideas using tool-augmented retrieval over MotivGraph and literature. Multi-round questioning without prescriptive answers mitigates confirmation bias and yields higher-quality proposals with accept/reject gating.
paper_title: MotivGraph-SoIQ: Integrating Motivational Knowledge Graphs and Socratic Dialogue for Enhanced LLM Ideation

problem: Building a scalable, structured motivational knowledge base from literature is labor-intensive and prone to inconsistency.
method: Create SciMotivMiner to automatically extract and canonicalize (problem, challenge, solution) triples from papers with strict naming rules, deduplicate/merge identical nodes, and add hierarchical parent nodes using embedding similarity plus LLM adjudication, producing a navigable, multi-level motivational KG for ideation.
paper_title: MotivGraph-SoIQ: Integrating Motivational Knowledge Graphs and Socratic Dialogue for Enhanced LLM Ideation

problem: Existing training data attribution (TDA) methods for autoregressive LMs (e.g., TracIn, Simfluence) struggle to accurately attribute per-example influence over training, generalize to unseen data, and are largely limited to test-loss prediction.
method: GPTfluence: a featurized, n-th order Markov simulator that models training dynamics with learned multiplicative/additive influence factors aggregated over each training batch. It uses frozen pre-trained text encoders (e.g., BERT/GPT) to embed training and test examples and computes per-example factors via bilinear/Frobenius interactions, enabling autoregressive prediction of full performance trajectories and robust generalization to unseen data.
paper_title: On Training Data Influence of GPT Models

problem: TDA for GPTs rarely covers downstream generative metrics (e.g., BLEU, ROUGE) and cannot simulate their evolution across training curricula.
method: Train the featurized simulator on logged training dynamics to directly predict arbitrary test metrics (loss, BLEU, ROUGE) step-by-step, outperforming gradient-based and linear simulators across instruction-tuning and fine-tuning and across model sizes (14M–2.8B).
paper_title: On Training Data Influence of GPT Models

problem: Research on data influence for GPTs is hindered by the lack of public, large-scale training-dynamics datasets.
method: Release GPTDynamics: >350 training runs over six GPT sizes and five tasks (NLU/NLG) with per-step curricula and metric trajectories, supporting benchmarking and training of influence simulators.
paper_title: On Training Data Influence of GPT Models

problem: RAG pipelines are bottlenecked by weaker dense retrievers and rigid, human-designed iterative workflows; LLMs are not trained to interleave reasoning with retrieval or decide when to stop.
method: R3-RAG formulates interleaved reasoning–retrieval as an RL problem where an LLM generates stepwise analyses, retrieval queries, and a final answer, interacting with an external retriever. The policy is optimized with PPO over trajectories defined by (reasoning, query, retrieved docs, answer), enabling the model to explore and learn effective multi-step retrieval plans.
paper_title: R3-RAG: Learning Step-by-Step Reasoning and Retrieval for LLMs via Reinforcement Learning

problem: Using only final-answer correctness yields sparse and noisy RL signals (string-match brittleness), hindering learning of high-quality retrieval steps.
method: A shaped reward that combines: (i) an outcome reward using a composite answer-correctness signal (string match + LLM-based judgment), (ii) a process reward via LLM-based relevance scoring of retrieved documents to the question at each step, and (iii) a format-correctness penalty. A trajectory-level weighting scheme amplifies process rewards on correct trajectories and down-weights them on incorrect ones to stabilize learning.
paper_title: R3-RAG: Learning Step-by-Step Reasoning and Retrieval for LLMs via Reinforcement Learning

problem: From-scratch RL struggles to learn long-horizon interleaved reasoning–retrieval behavior; base LLMs lack the interaction schema.
method: A cold-start supervised stage that auto-generates multi-step trajectories with prompt templates, followed by step-conditioned SFT to teach the interleaving schema (analysis → query or answer). This initialization improves positive-trajectory sampling and makes subsequent PPO training more effective.
paper_title: R3-RAG: Learning Step-by-Step Reasoning and Retrieval for LLMs via Reinforcement Learning

problem: Safety-tuned LLMs like ChatGPT largely resist direct prompts and simple jailbreaks for extracting personally identifiable information (PII).
method: A Multi-step Jailbreaking Prompt (MJP) that stages a three-turn dialogue: (1) user issues a role-play jailbreak, (2) assistant ‘acknowledges’ jailbreak mode, and (3) user asks for PII while inviting a ‘random guess’ if unsure. This multi-turn, morality-undermining setup reliably bypasses guardrails and elicits memorized PII (notably emails), outperforming direct and single-step jailbreak prompts.
paper_title: Multi-step Jailbreaking Privacy Attacks on ChatGPT

problem: Privacy risks from application-integrated LLMs (e.g., LLM + web search) are underexplored and may amplify PII exposure beyond model memorization.
method: Two attack modes against New Bing: (a) partially identified extraction using direct prompts (name + domain) and (b) free-form extraction using only domain cues. Leveraging the integrated search-and-summarize pipeline, the system aggregates and outputs (name, PII) pairs with high accuracy (e.g., 94% institutional emails), even deobfuscating contact info, enabling near-zero-cost PII harvesting.
paper_title: Multi-step Jailbreaking Privacy Attacks on ChatGPT

problem: Black-box LLM outputs are noisy, limiting reliable PII extraction from a single generation.
method: Response verification that samples multiple generations and then (i) reframes candidates as a multiple-choice question for the LLM to select (LLM-as-judge) or (ii) applies majority voting. This selection layer boosts extraction accuracy (e.g., +~10% on Enron emails) without accessing model internals.
paper_title: Multi-step Jailbreaking Privacy Attacks on ChatGPT

problem: Efficiently remove the influence of specific training data from large language models on demand without retraining the whole model, while preserving task performance and reducing the ability to extract forgotten information.
method: Introduce lightweight unlearning layers (parameter-efficient adapters inserted after transformer FFNs) and train only these via a selective teacher–student objective: minimize KL divergence to the frozen teacher on retained data and maximize it on forgotten data; add task loss on retained data to preserve utility and a negated LM (masked LM) loss on forgotten data to erase memorized content; optimize via alternating retain/forget updates, tuning ~0.5% of parameters.
paper_title: Unlearn What You Want to Forget: Efficient Unlearning for LLMs

problem: Support dynamic, sequential unlearning requests by combining multiple unlearning operations into a single model without extra retraining or storing raw forgotten data.
method: Fuse separately learned unlearning layers into one unified adapter using a closed-form linear regression that matches each layer’s outputs on its corresponding forgotten set, computed from pre-stored inner-product matrices of hidden representations; this performs merging without additional training and preserves privacy by avoiding storage of the raw data.
paper_title: Unlearn What You Want to Forget: Efficient Unlearning for LLMs

problem: Instance-level LLM-text detectors are unreliable, brittle across models/domains, and computationally expensive; stakeholders need a stable way to measure how much of a large corpus is AI-generated or substantially AI-modified.
method: Distributional GPT quantification: model the target corpus as a P–Q mixture and estimate the AI fraction α via maximum likelihood, using reference human (P) and LLM (Q) corpora to learn token-occurrence distributions. A simple compound model over stylistic tokens (notably adjectives) computes document/sentence likelihoods without classifying instances, yielding >10^7× lower inference cost and 3–5× lower estimation error than SOTA detectors in- and out-of-distribution.
paper_title: Monitoring AI-Modified Content at Scale: A Case Study on the Impact of ChatGPT on AI Conference Peer Reviews

problem: Directly estimating document distributions is intractable and detectors often overfit to content/topic shifts; we need a robust, simple signal that distinguishes human vs LLM style and is stable across prompts and venues.
method: Parametrize P and Q by occurrence probabilities of a curated vocabulary of adjectives that LLMs disproportionately use, learned from expert-written reviews (pre-ChatGPT) and AI reviews generated from official review instructions. Validate with semi-synthetic mixes, prompt-shift tests, and show robustness to mere proofreading while remaining sensitive to substantial LLM expansions of human outlines.
paper_title: Monitoring AI-Modified Content at Scale: A Case Study on the Impact of ChatGPT on AI Conference Peer Reviews

problem: The impact of LLM use on peer-review ecosystems is unclear; we need corpus-level quantification and behavioral effects beyond anecdotal detection.
method: Apply the estimator to ICLR/NeurIPS/CoRL/EMNLP and Nature journals to quantify post-ChatGPT AI-modification rates (≈6.5%–16.9% in ML venues; no significant rise in Nature). Analyze correlates—deadline proximity, lower reviewer confidence, fewer author replies, fewer citations—and show homogenization via embedding-space convergence, revealing systemic shifts too subtle for instance-level detection.
paper_title: Monitoring AI-Modified Content at Scale: A Case Study on the Impact of ChatGPT on AI Conference Peer Reviews

problem: LLM-generated text detectors are brittle to paraphrasing and adaptive attacks, and improving robustness often requires model retraining.
method: OUTFOX detector: a retrieval-augmented, attack-aware in-context learning approach where a detector LLM classifies essays using prompts that mix semantically retrieved examples of human, non-attacked LM, and adversarially generated essays (attacks labeled as LM), enabling robustness without parameter updates and yielding large F1 gains on attacked texts while maintaining state-of-the-art performance on clean texts.
paper_title: OUTFOX: LLM-Generated Essay Detection Through In-Context Learning with Adversarially Generated Examples

problem: Paraphrasing-based attacks fail to reliably evade detectors and do not adapt to specific detector behaviors.
method: OUTFOX attacker: an in-context adversarial essay generator that conditions on the detector’s predicted labels for semantically similar prompts and is explicitly instructed to produce essays classified as Human with matched length, yielding attacks that are semantically closer to human essays and drastically reducing detector F1 (up to −57 points), outperforming paraphrasing (DIPPER).
paper_title: OUTFOX: LLM-Generated Essay Detection Through In-Context Learning with Adversarially Generated Examples

problem: Robust evaluation of detector–attacker dynamics is limited by the lack of realistic, labeled datasets of native-student essays aligned with prompts.
method: Construct a dataset of 15,400 triplets (problem statement, human-written essay, LLM-generated essay from ChatGPT/GPT-3.5/FLAN-T5-XXL) plus attacked sets; use it to train/evaluate OUTFOX and baselines, and release code/data for reproducibility.
paper_title: OUTFOX: LLM-Generated Essay Detection Through In-Context Learning with Adversarially Generated Examples

problem: Prompt sensitivity of LLMs is typically assessed only at the dataset/template level, masking instance-level variability and ignoring subjective (human-aligned) evaluations, making robustness hard to measure accurately.
method: Introduce ProSA with PromptSensiScore (PSS), an instance-level metric that computes the average pairwise discrepancy of model performance across semantically equivalent prompt variants. PSS is defined for both objective tasks (correctness) and subjective tasks (LLM-judge quality scores), enabling fine-grained, cross-task robustness assessment.
paper_title: ProSA: Assessing and Understanding the Prompt Sensitivity of LLMs

problem: The underlying mechanism of why LLMs are sensitive to prompt wording is unclear, limiting diagnostic power and principled mitigation.
method: Leverage decoding confidence (token probabilities) to explain sensitivity: compute instance-level confidence from next-token probabilities and show a strong inverse correlation with PSS; conclude that prompt sensitivity is an external manifestation of low model confidence, providing a mechanistic interpretation.
paper_title: ProSA: Assessing and Understanding the Prompt Sensitivity of LLMs

problem: Practitioners lack actionable guidance on reducing prompt sensitivity and understanding when it arises across models and tasks.
method: Use ProSA to run a broad empirical study across models, tasks, and prompt styles, revealing that (i) larger models are generally more robust, (ii) adding few-shot examples markedly reduces sensitivity—especially from 0-shot to 1-shot—and (iii) category effects matter: knowledge-heavy queries are robust while creative/reasoning-heavy tasks (e.g., coding, data visualization) are more sensitive; these findings offer concrete mitigation and evaluation guidance.
paper_title: ProSA: Assessing and Understanding the Prompt Sensitivity of LLMs

problem: Zero-shot VLN agents that rely on a single model's one-pass self-thinking struggle to jointly parse instructions, perceive the scene, track progress, and plan actions, leading to weak sim-to-real performance.
method: DiscussNav: a stepwise, consultative inference paradigm where an LLM-driven agent actively queries multiple domain experts before every move. It uses a structured question corpus to gather instruction analysis, vision perception, and progress signals, then performs chain-of-thought with beam search to decide actions.
paper_title: Discuss Before Moving: Visual Language Navigation via Multi-expert Discussions

problem: VLN requires diverse, subtask-specific expertise (action/landmark parsing, scene/object perception, progress tracking) without task-specific training or human annotations.
method: Prompt-instantiated domain experts built entirely from large models: GPT-4/ChatGPT for action decomposition, landmark extraction, trajectory summarization, and completion estimation; InstructBLIP for scene-level queries with type-guided prompts to curb hallucinations; RAM for open-vocabulary object tagging across 12 directions—activated and coordinated via role/task prompts.
paper_title: Discuss Before Moving: Visual Language Navigation via Multi-expert Discussions

problem: Stepwise reasoning accumulates errors and produces inconsistent movement predictions across beams, reducing reliability.
method: Built-in verification and arbitration: (i) completion estimation that tracks executed/in-progress/to-execute actions using trajectory summaries and structured CoT checks; (ii) decision testing with thought fusion and feasibility evaluation to select the most reliable beam—filtering inconsistent or erroneous moves.
paper_title: Discuss Before Moving: Visual Language Navigation via Multi-expert Discussions

problem: Self-supervised vision pre-training lacks a simple, scalable objective with predictable gains from more data/parameters and a training signal that tracks downstream quality.
method: AIM: train Vision Transformers autoregressively to predict the next image patch in raster order using per-patch normalized pixel regression, scaling width-heavy ViTs from 0.6B to 7B parameters on 2B+ uncurated images (DFN-2B+). Training uses uniform hyperparameters without stability tricks and exhibits LLM-like scaling: pre-training loss correlates with frozen-transfer performance and shows no saturation (e.g., 84.0% ImageNet-1k with a frozen trunk).
paper_title: Scalable Pre-training of Large Autoregressive Image Models

problem: Causal attention used in autoregressive pre-training mismatches the bidirectional attention preferred for downstream recognition, degrading transfer when the mask is kept or removed.
method: Prefix attention (PrefixLM-style) during pre-training: uniformly sample a prefix length S, make the prefix bidirectional and exclude it from the AR loss, while keeping the rest causal. This enables dropping the causal mask at adaptation time and yields better frozen-transfer performance.
paper_title: Scalable Pre-training of Large Autoregressive Image Models

problem: Pixel-level autoregressive prediction can over-specialize the backbone to low-level signals, hurting feature transferability.
method: Attach a heavily parameterized per-token MLP prediction head (multiple deep MLP blocks) above the ViT trunk so the head models low-level pixel details while the trunk learns higher-level semantics. This consistently improves downstream results over no head or a transformer head, with depth providing the largest gains.
paper_title: Scalable Pre-training of Large Autoregressive Image Models

problem: LLMs/MLLMs used for document understanding underutilize layout structure, limiting zero-shot performance and layout comprehension.
method: Introduce layout instruction tuning with a document-aware encoder (LayoutLMv3) and dual projectors, and pre-train across three hierarchical levels unified as instructions: (1) document-level Dense Description (long, rich captions) and Text & Layout Reconstruction that outputs <box, text> to align layout/text embeddings to the LLM space; (2) region-level Document Layout Analysis (region localization/type) and Table Understanding (rows/cols, logical coordinates, cell content); (3) segment-level masked V+L modeling, position masking, and geometric relation queries framed as QA.
paper_title: LayoutLLM: Layout Instruction Tuning with Large Language Models for Document Understanding

problem: Standard supervised fine-tuning directly on answers ignores layout-aware reasoning and fails to focus on relevant regions, hurting accuracy and interpretability.
method: Propose LayoutCoT, a layout-grounded chain-of-thought with three steps: Question Analysis (infer task/layout type), Relevant Area Concentration (predict bounding boxes of pertinent regions), and Answer Formation (region-specific reasoning, e.g., table row/column logic). This guides attention to the right areas, improves zero-shot VQA/VIE, and enables interpretable, human-in-the-loop correction.
paper_title: LayoutLLM: Layout Instruction Tuning with Large Language Models for Document Understanding

problem: Obtaining layout-aware CoT annotations is costly and scarce for large-scale SFT.
method: Develop a manual-label-free pipeline using GPT-3.5: represent documents with layout text or HTML, generate QA and textual CoT, then convert to LayoutCoT by mapping referenced sentences to union bounding boxes; render HTML/MRC to images. Produces ~300K layout-aware SFT instructions to train LayoutCoT without human labeling.
paper_title: LayoutLLM: Layout Instruction Tuning with Large Language Models for Document Understanding

problem: Practitioners default to chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting, but there is no principled way to predict when CoT will harm model performance.
method: Introduce a psychology-grounded heuristic: identify task archetypes where human verbal deliberation impairs performance and test whether CoT similarly degrades LLM/LMM accuracy. Adapt six classic cognitive tasks and show large CoT-induced drops in three archetypes—implicit statistical learning, verbal overshadowing in face recognition, and category learning with exceptions—establishing when inference-time reasoning is risky.
paper_title: Mind Your Step (by Step): Chain-of-Thought can Reduce Performance on Tasks where Thinking Makes Humans Worse

problem: There is no scalable, standardized evaluation suite to stress-test CoT on tasks known to trigger human overthinking failures.
method: Construct and release a "human overthinking" benchmark by massively scaling six psychology tasks (e.g., 4,400 artificial-grammar judgments over 100 FSGs; 500 face-matching problems across 2,500 synthetic faces; 2,400 vehicle items with exception-laden labels). Evaluate many state-of-the-art LLMs/LMMs under zero-shot vs CoT (and ToT/steering ablations), quantifying substantial degradations (e.g., up to −36.3% absolute) and showing ToT and generic CoT steering fail to recover zero-shot performance by default.
paper_title: Mind Your Step (by Step): Chain-of-Thought can Reduce Performance on Tasks where Thinking Makes Humans Worse

problem: We lack clear boundary conditions explaining why CoT sometimes helps instead of hurts, and no evidence that a psychology-based heuristic is systematically better than chance at finding CoT failures.
method: Run comparative analyses on three additional tasks (logical inconsistency, spatial/motor intuition, long-context preference aggregation) to attribute mixed CoT effects to model–human differences (e.g., logical priors, lack of motor simulation, large context windows). Validate the heuristic’s efficiency via bootstrap against a meta-study (378 comparisons), showing it finds larger and more frequent CoT-induced drops than random task selection.
paper_title: Mind Your Step (by Step): Chain-of-Thought can Reduce Performance on Tasks where Thinking Makes Humans Worse

problem: LLMs catastrophically forget prior skills during continual instruction tuning when original training data from earlier stages is unavailable (e.g., public checkpoints).
method: Self-Synthesized Rehearsal (SSR): use the base LLM’s in-context learning to synthesize rehearsal instances from a few demonstrations (which can be tiny subsets or human-written), sampling/reordering to create diverse pairs; crucially, use the base LLM (not the latest fine-tuned model) to avoid ICL degradation after SFT.
paper_title: Mitigating Catastrophic Forgetting in Large Language Models with Self-Synthesized Rehearsal

problem: Synthetic rehearsal outputs can be noisy and may not reflect the latest model’s acquired capabilities, weakening rehearsal efficacy.
method: Synthetic Output Refinement: for each synthesized input, regenerate the output with the latest LLM to align rehearsal targets with its current predictive distribution, reducing noise and better preserving acquired knowledge across stages.
paper_title: Mitigating Catastrophic Forgetting in Large Language Models with Self-Synthesized Rehearsal

problem: With limited rehearsal budget, synthetic data may be redundant or low quality, reducing data efficiency.
method: Diversity- and quality-aware selection: cluster synthetic instances (e.g., K-means on embeddings) and select samples near cluster centroids for replay, yielding a compact, diverse rehearsal set that works without access to real past data.
paper_title: Mitigating Catastrophic Forgetting in Large Language Models with Self-Synthesized Rehearsal

problem: Automated multi-agent systems typically search for a single, static workflow that cannot adapt to query difficulty/domain, leading to over-complex pipelines and wasted inference resources.
method: Introduce an agentic supernet—a probabilistic, multi-layer distribution over agentic operators (e.g., CoT, Debate, ReAct). A controller network conditionally samples a query-specific architecture using MoE-style gating over lightweight embeddings of the query and previously chosen operators, activates a variable number of operators per layer, and supports an early-exit operator to stop when simple solutions suffice, enabling dynamic resource allocation.
paper_title: Multi-agent Architecture Search via Agentic Supernet

problem: Optimizing multi-agent workflows is non-differentiable (API/tool calls) and cost-insensitive, making it hard to jointly improve performance while controlling token/API costs.
method: Train the supernet with a cost-constrained objective (utility minus λ·cost). Estimate gradients for the architecture distribution via empirical Bayes Monte Carlo with cost-aware importance weights, and update operator internals via agent-based textual gradients that propose edits to prompts, temperatures, and node structures—jointly evolving both routing and operators toward high performance at low token cost.
paper_title: Multi-agent Architecture Search via Agentic Supernet

problem: Searched agentic systems often overfit to a dataset or LLM backbone and struggle to incorporate new operators.
method: Model operators as modular nodes with LLM-generated profiles encoded by lightweight text embeddings for conditional routing. The supernet’s query- and history-conditioned gating yields robust cross-dataset/backbone transfer and inductive ability to utilize previously unseen operators at inference without retraining.
paper_title: Multi-agent Architecture Search via Agentic Supernet

problem: LLMs struggle to reason over large, noisy tables due to limited context windows and irrelevant content, leading to poor grounding for complex queries.
method: A table sampling module that selects a sub-table via embedding-based semantic relevance to the query with column grounding, diversity-preserving centroid clustering, and a hybrid scorer combining similarity and diversity; compared against rule-based and LLM-based baselines, the hybrid + column grounding sampling consistently yields better sub-table extraction for downstream reasoning.
paper_title: TAP4LLM: Table Provider on Sampling, Augmenting, and Packing Semi-structured Data for Large Language Model Reasoning

problem: Raw tables often contain ambiguous terms and miss implicit global context, causing misinterpretation and hallucination; sampling alone may discard essential signals.
method: A unified table augmentation suite that injects (i) metadata inferred by LLMs (dimension/measure, semantic field types, statistics, header hierarchy), (ii) retrieval-augmented knowledge (document references and LLM-guided term explanations via vector search), and (iii) self-consistency prompting; this modular augmentation reduces hallucination and improves comprehension across TQA/TFV/Text-to-SQL.
paper_title: TAP4LLM: Table Provider on Sampling, Augmenting, and Packing Semi-structured Data for Large Language Model Reasoning

problem: Packing both sampled tables and augmentations into prompts under tight token budgets and suboptimal serializations hurts LLM understanding and efficiency.
method: A table packing and serialization module that budgets tokens between sampled content and augmentation and serializes into formats (HTML/XML/JSON/CSV/MD/NL) suited to LLMs; an empirical guideline identifies near-optimal token allocation (≈5:5 or 4:6 table:augmentation) to maximize performance under fixed budgets.
paper_title: TAP4LLM: Table Provider on Sampling, Augmenting, and Packing Semi-structured Data for Large Language Model Reasoning

problem: Selecting high-quality instruction-tuning data for VLMs is unreliable with prior methods that use single heuristics or predefined evaluation tasks, risking overfitting and lacking true multimodal difficulty estimation.
method: SELF-FILTER Stage 1: co-train a lightweight score net with the target VLM. The score net ingests frozen multimodal features/scores (e.g., CLIP embeddings, GPT-4V/ChatGPT-derived scores) and outputs per-sample weights that reweight the VLM loss; inter-batch softmax normalization stabilizes scales. After training, difficulty is defined as the negative learned weight, yielding a task-agnostic, model-informed ranking without surrogate models or external eval sets.
paper_title: Your Vision-Language Model Itself Is a Strong Filter: Towards High-Quality Instruction Tuning with Data Selection

problem: Choosing only hard samples can create redundancy (topic clumping) and hurt generalization due to low diversity in the pruned set.
method: SELF-FILTER Stage 2: iteratively select highest-difficulty samples and apply a KNN-based diversity penalty in CLIP feature space, downweighting k-nearest neighbors via cosine similarity to promote semantic/topic coverage.
paper_title: Your Vision-Language Model Itself Is a Strong Filter: Towards High-Quality Instruction Tuning with Data Selection

problem: Video inpainting relies on tedious, manually labeled masks and lacks a dataset pairing videos with language instructions and ground-truth inpainted results, especially for interactive dialogue-based guidance.
method: ROVI dataset: constructs triplets of original videos, referring/interactive expressions, and inpainted videos by augmenting referring video segmentation datasets (with masks and expressions) using a SOTA video inpainting model across mask dilations with human selection; enriches with LLM/MLLM-generated chat-style dialogues and responses to support both referring and interactive language-driven video inpainting.
paper_title: Towards Language-Driven Video Inpainting via Multimodal Large Language Models

problem: Need an end-to-end model that performs language-driven video inpainting with temporal consistency and precise region targeting, without requiring masks at inference.
method: LGVI diffusion framework: inflates a pre-trained text-to-image latent diffusion U-Net to video via temporal inflation; adds a lightweight Temporal Attention module between cross-attention and FFN for time consistency; conditions on original video latents concatenated with noised target latents; introduces a mask decoder trained with mask supervision to focus generation on language-referred regions; jointly trains on images and videos for robustness.
paper_title: Towards Language-Driven Video Inpainting via Multimodal Large Language Models

problem: Language-driven inpainting must understand complex, conversational requests and translate them into effective guidance for video generation.
method: LGVI-I: integrates an MLLM (e.g., LLaVA) that reads frames and chat input to produce responses and special <PROMPT>…</PROMPT> spans; extracts the hidden states between these markers via an MM head to form vision-aware prompt embeddings, which feed the U-Net cross-attention; trains end-to-end with diffusion and language modeling losses to execute interactive instructions effectively.
paper_title: Towards Language-Driven Video Inpainting via Multimodal Large Language Models

problem: Existing 3D HOI datasets provide only coarse, sequence-level goal captions and lack state-level semantics and transition descriptions, hindering fine-grained alignment between language and HOI states.
method: Construct Semantic-HOI by unifying GRAB, CHAIRS, and BEHAVE into 20k+ paired HOI states with fine-grained, part-level state captions and state-to-state movement descriptions generated via structured GPT-4V prompts and manual verification, ensuring consistency across 2D images, 3D HOI poses, object meshes, and text.
paper_title: F-HOI: Toward Fine-grained Semantic-Aligned 3D Human-Object Interactions

problem: There is no unified formulation or model to align fine-grained language with 2D/3D HOI across understanding, reasoning, generation, and reconstruction tasks.
method: Propose F-HOI, a unified MLLM framework that encodes multimodal inputs (CLIP for images, Uni3D for meshes, projectors for human/object poses, and text) into a shared language space of Vicuna-7B (LoRA-tuned), uses instruction-based prompts to perform state-level understanding, next-state reasoning, and next-state generation, and decodes poses via special tokens with task-specific projectors to maintain cross-space (2D/3D/text) consistency.
paper_title: F-HOI: Toward Fine-grained Semantic-Aligned 3D Human-Object Interactions

problem: Learning fine-grained semantic alignment from limited HOI data and making pose generation follow movement text is challenging.
method: Adopt a multi-stage training strategy: pretrain image-to-pose (COCO) and text-to-pose (PoseScript) alignments, then multi-task instruction-tune on Semantic-HOI; introduce offset regression to predict human/object pose deltas rather than absolute poses, improving adherence to movement descriptions and boosting generation and reconstruction performance.
paper_title: F-HOI: Toward Fine-grained Semantic-Aligned 3D Human-Object Interactions

problem: LoRA-based PEFT still overfits, yet existing transformer-specific dropouts (DropKey, DropAttention, HiddenCut) were designed for full finetuning and lack a unified understanding of their behavior under LoRA.
method: Theoretically and empirically compare these methods, proving DropKey and DropAttention have equivalent forward passes (with normalized rescaling) while DropAttention’s NoGrad introduces gradient noise in backprop; distill a unified framework with three axes—dropping position, structural pattern, and compensation—to systematize design and analysis under LoRA.
paper_title: LoRA Meets Dropout under a Unified Framework

problem: There is no dropout method tailored to LoRA that jointly regularizes attention and MLP while closing the train–inference gap.
method: Propose HiddenKey: column-wise dropping of attention logits (DropKey-style before softmax) plus element-wise dropping of hidden representations (HiddenCut-style), augmented with bidirectional KL loss between two stochastic forward passes to reduce the training–inference gap; achieves consistent gains across NLU and NLG (RoBERTa-large, GPT2-Medium, LLaMA2-7B).
paper_title: LoRA Meets Dropout under a Unified Framework

problem: Optimal dropout patterns and compensation under LoRA are unclear, and prior full-finetuning preferences (e.g., span-wise HiddenCut, JS consistency) may not hold.
method: Within the unified framework, run targeted ablations showing new preferences in LoRA: DropKey performs best with column-wise masks; HiddenCut prefers element-wise over span/column; JS consistency is ineffective while bidirectional KL consistently helps; combining DropKey and HiddenCut outperforms any single method.
paper_title: LoRA Meets Dropout under a Unified Framework

problem: Knowledge distillation is mostly used for model compression; it’s unclear whether it can improve generalization when student and teacher have identical capacity and how to reliably surpass a trained network of the same size.
method: Born-Again Networks (BAN): train a fresh student with the same architecture to match the teacher’s softmax outputs via cross-entropy (often without temperature and even without ground-truth labels), iterate for multiple generations, and optionally ensemble generations (BANE). This self-distillation consistently yields students that outperform their teachers (SOTA on CIFAR-10/100).
paper_title: Born-Again Neural Networks

problem: What components of the KD signal drive gains—non-argmax ‘dark knowledge’ vs. reweighting by teacher confidence?
method: Analyze the KD gradient and introduce two objectives: (i) Confidence-Weighted by Teacher Max (CWTM), which uses standard label loss weighted by the teacher’s max probability (removing non-argmax info), and (ii) Dark Knowledge with Permuted Predictions (DKPP), which permutes non-argmax logits to destroy class-specific similarities while preserving higher-order moments. Empirical results show both treatments still help, isolating the roles of importance weighting and distributional structure beyond specific class relations.
paper_title: Born-Again Neural Networks

problem: Can knowledge be transferred effectively across architectures of similar capacity and under structural changes (depth/width/compression) to improve weaker or different models?
method: Apply BAN across architectures and variants: DenseNet→ResNet and ResNet→DenseNet students (matching stage output shapes and sometimes sharing first/last layers) and DenseNet students with varied depth/width/compression. Students often surpass their teachers, demonstrating cross-architecture self-distillation and robustness to architectural variation; even weaker masters can improve students.
paper_title: Born-Again Neural Networks

problem: Vision–language models (e.g., VQA) lack explicit, high-quality supervision that links textual questions/answers to the precise visual regions responsible for the answer, limiting attention learning and grounding.
method: Create VQS, a dataset that links VQA question–answer pairs on COCO images to human-selected instance segmentations (and tight boxes when needed) that visually answer the question, transferring segmentation supervision to VQA and enabling grounded training.
paper_title: VQS: Linking Segmentations to Questions and Answers for Supervised Attention in VQA and Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation

problem: Robots lack a benchmark to learn following high-level natural-language commands to navigate unseen real indoor environments and precisely ground remote target objects; existing VLN focuses on path-following and RefExp on single images, missing object-level localisation after navigation.
method: Introduce the REVERIE task and dataset: extend Matterport3D with object-level annotations and dynamic 2D bounding boxes projected from 3D across viewpoints with occlusion/visibility handling; provide 21,702 concise human instructions over 4,140 target objects in 90 buildings and evaluation metrics for both navigation and object localisation (IoU ≥ 0.5 within 3m).
paper_title: REVERIE: Remote Embodied Visual Referring Expression in Real Indoor Environments

problem: Naively combining state-of-the-art navigation and referring expression models performs poorly because navigation and grounding are not coordinated during decision making.
method: Propose an Interactive Navigator-Pointer model: a FAST-based navigator (seq2seq with attention/backtracking) interacts with a MAttNet pointer (subject/location/relation modules). An interaction module feeds the navigator with top-3 grounded object labels (BiLSTM-encoded) and visual features (averaged FC7) per candidate viewpoint as hard attention, and trains jointly with navigation (action CE + progress monitor) and grounding ranking losses, yielding improved success on unseen environments.
paper_title: REVERIE: Remote Embodied Visual Referring Expression in Real Indoor Environments

problem: Attention mechanisms in VQA are typically trained as latent variables with weak signals (text-only answers), leading to poor alignment with human attention and suboptimal performance.
method: Supervise attention using VQS: downsample linked segmentation masks to region grids to form target attention distributions and train an attention network with cross-entropy; augment a strong MLP VQA baseline with these attention features (and attribute features), achieving state-of-the-art results on VQA Real Multiple-Choice and outperforming human attention maps and bounding-box supervision.
paper_title: VQS: Linking Segmentations to Questions and Answers for Supervised Attention in VQA and Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation

problem: VQA outputs text and can exploit shortcuts without producing visual evidence; there is no established task/method to output pixel-wise evidence that answers a question.
method: Introduce Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation (QFSS) and propose a mask aggregation model: generate segmentation proposals (SharpMask), learn question-conditioned softmax weights over proposal features, and aggregate them into a final mask via convex combination; benchmark against a text-conditioned DeconvNet baseline and an oracle upper bound using ground-truth COCO instance masks.
paper_title: VQS: Linking Segmentations to Questions and Answers for Supervised Attention in VQA and Question-Focused Semantic Segmentation

problem: End-to-end VQA systems are opaque and often fail on questions that require external/common-sense knowledge beyond visual recognition.
method: Add an explicit Probabilistic Soft Logic (PSL) reasoning layer that fuses noisy evidence from three sources—visual relation triplets parsed from dense image captions, a semantic parse of the question, and background ontological/distributional knowledge (ConceptNet, word2vec)—and performs phrasal-similarity-aware soft logical inference to select answers and provide interpretable evidential predicates.
paper_title: Explicit Reasoning over End-to-End Neural Architectures for Visual Question Answering

problem: Extracting structured, relation-level semantics from images and questions is hard due to open-ended relations and noisy language, limiting downstream reasoning.
method: A heuristic semantic parsing pipeline that converts dense captions and questions into open-ended relation triplets using dependency shortest paths and phrase-similarity matching to a large Visual Genome relation set, with confidence scores; additionally, release annotated Visual Genome subsets (word-pairs and relations) to seed semi-supervised parsing.
paper_title: Explicit Reasoning over End-to-End Neural Architectures for Visual Question Answering

problem: Standard PSL does not directly handle phrasal similarity or rank a small set of candidate answers under uncertainty.
method: Augment PSL with similarity predicates derived from word2vec and ConceptNet and impose a summation constraint over answer variables to perform MAP inference that concentrates mass on high-confidence answers; learn rule weights via maximum likelihood, improving ‘what/which’ VQA while yielding interpretable evidence.
paper_title: Explicit Reasoning over End-to-End Neural Architectures for Visual Question Answering

problem: Deep networks typically output fixed-size vectors or rely on sequential/independent modules, making them ill-suited for predicting orderless sets with unknown cardinality (e.g., multi-label classification).
method: Formulate set prediction via finite set statistics with an i.i.d.-cluster point process and learn both the set cardinality and element (state) distributions jointly within a single deep network. Use a Dirichlet-Categorical prior for cardinality and Bernoulli/logistic outputs for labels, trained end-to-end via a MAP objective that ties both tasks through shared weights.
paper_title: Joint Learning of Set Cardinality and State Distribution

problem: Inference for set outputs often depends on ad-hoc thresholds or fixed k, yielding non-optimal and potentially invalid sets.
method: Derive a permutation-invariant MAP inference that jointly selects cardinality and elements: choose m to maximize a closed-form objective combining sorted label scores and the Dirichlet-Categorical prior, then take the top-m labels. This yields an efficient, globally optimal, and valid set prediction without heuristics.
paper_title: Joint Learning of Set Cardinality and State Distribution

problem: Common cardinality models (Poisson/negative binomial) have variance coupled to the mean and are ill-suited for bounded-cardinality tasks like image tagging.
method: Model cardinality with a categorical distribution and a conjugate Dirichlet prior (Dirichlet-Categorical), enabling closed-form marginalization and a stable loss that integrates seamlessly with the joint network, improving cardinality estimation and overall multi-label performance (SOTA on PASCAL VOC and MS COCO).
paper_title: Joint Learning of Set Cardinality and State Distribution

problem: Sampled softmax for large-class classification is biased unless sampling matches the softmax distribution, and practical samplers (uniform/popularity) need many negatives; we need an adaptive, per-example, efficient sampler close to softmax that works with complex models.
method: Kernel-based sampling q_i ∝ K(h, w_i) where K is a non-negative kernel with feature map φ, enabling the partition function to be a dot product with a precomputed summary z=∑φ(w_j). A divide-and-conquer tree sampler draws classes in O(D log n), supports updates in O(D log n), and reduces memory from O(nD) to O(nd) via high-branching leaves (size O(Dd)) with the final step computed in the original space; applicable to any dot-product last layer.
paper_title: Adaptive Sampled Softmax with Kernel Based Sampling

problem: Design a practical kernel that approximates softmax-based importance while avoiding oversampling negatives and ensuring unbiasedness compatibility with a symmetric kernel.
method: Use a quadratic kernel K(h, w)=α⟨h, w⟩^2+1 (feature dimension D=O(d^2)) for sampling, paired with an 'absolute softmax' prediction p_i ∝ exp(|o_i|) to align unbiasedness conditions for symmetric kernels while preserving expressiveness via softmax shift-invariance; yields O(d^2 log n) sampling.
paper_title: Adaptive Sampled Softmax with Kernel Based Sampling

problem: Quantify how sampler choice and negative sample size affect bias and convergence in practice.
method: Empirical evaluation on Penn Treebank language modeling and large-scale YouTube recommendation (10k/100k classes) shows quadratic-kernel sampling attains near full-softmax quality with 10–100× fewer negatives than uniform, and once bias is reduced, increasing the number of negatives does not appreciably speed convergence.
paper_title: Adaptive Sampled Softmax with Kernel Based Sampling

problem: Two-stage open-vocabulary segmentation relies on CLIP to classify masked region proposals, but pre-trained CLIP performs poorly on masked crops due to domain shift; naively fine-tuning on closed-set segmentation labels harms open-vocabulary generalization.
method: Finetune CLIP’s image encoder on masked-region/text pairs mined from image captions via a self-labeling pipeline: extract caption nouns, generate class-agnostic mask proposals, and use CLIP to align each noun with its best-matching proposal. This creates a large, diverse (noisy) training set that adapts CLIP to masked images while preserving open-vocabulary generalization (text encoder kept frozen).
paper_title: Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation with Mask-adapted CLIP

problem: Masked images introduce many blank (zero) tokens to CLIP’s visual transformer, causing distribution shift and degraded classification; practical settings may also require adapting performance without changing shared CLIP weights.
method: Mask Prompt Tuning (MPT): during tokenization, replace zero tokens from fully masked patches with learnable prompt tokens (optionally at multiple transformer depths). Train only the prompts or combine with full fine-tuning, yielding significant gains on masked inputs while enabling parameter-efficient adaptation and further improving a fully fine-tuned model.
paper_title: Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation with Mask-adapted CLIP

problem: Designing multimodal models requires manual selection of which intermediate unimodal features to fuse (often limited to inter-modal or fixed topologies), leading to suboptimal and task-specific solutions.
method: A bilevel, cell-by-cell differentiable NAS: the upper level searches a DAG of Cells that select inter- and intra-modal feature pairs from pretrained backbones via continuous relaxation (α), reducing combinatorial complexity versus pairwise search.
paper_title: BM-NAS: Bilevel Multimodal Neural Architecture Search

problem: Existing differentiable NAS frameworks don’t support multi-input fusion operations and thus cannot discover rich fusion modules.
method: A lower-level search over bivariate primitive operations (Zero, Sum, Attention with guided Q/KV, LinearGLU, ConcatFC) with continuous weights (β, γ), allowing flexible compositions that instantiate known modules (e.g., multi-head attention, AoA) or novel fusions; repeated inputs enable multi-head structures.
paper_title: BM-NAS: Bilevel Multimodal Neural Architecture Search

problem: Generalized multimodal NAS methods are either black-box (high search cost) or restrict ops/topology, and hand-crafted fusion layers inflate parameters.
method: End-to-end differentiable optimization of architecture (α, β, γ) and weights in a shared-weights hypernet (DARTS-style), yielding 10x faster search and compact fusion models that match or surpass SOTA across diverse multimodal tasks.
paper_title: BM-NAS: Bilevel Multimodal Neural Architecture Search

problem: Early exiting in unified encoder–decoder vision–language models cannot effectively skip encoder layers because intermediate encoder states lack task-confidence signals, leading to suboptimal compute savings and limited dynamic allocation across inputs.
method: MuE triggers early exit in both encoder and decoder using layer-wise cosine similarity as a saturation proxy: when similarity exceeds a threshold, remaining layers are skipped. This confidence-free criterion supports multiple exits per example and includes a decay schedule for decoding timesteps, enabling dynamic compute allocation without auxiliary classifiers.
paper_title: You Need Multiple Exiting: Dynamic Early Exiting for Accelerating Unified Vision Language Model

problem: Early-fusion encoders entangle image and text, so uniform layer skipping harms key modalities and wastes opportunities for task-adaptive savings.
method: Modality decomposition duplicates the encoder into tied-weight, modality-specific image and text encoders with respective positional biases, processes tokens independently, applies per-modality early exit, then concatenates outputs for the decoder—allowing selective skipping of less-critical modality layers per task while preserving performance.
paper_title: You Need Multiple Exiting: Dynamic Early Exiting for Accelerating Unified Vision Language Model

problem: Exiting decoder layers early degrades accuracy because intermediate representations are not trained to produce final outputs, and errors accumulate in autoregressive generation.
method: Layer-wise task loss supervises every decoder layer with the task objective (e.g., cross-entropy), training intermediate layers to be predictive and reducing error accumulation, thereby maintaining performance under aggressive early exiting.
paper_title: You Need Multiple Exiting: Dynamic Early Exiting for Accelerating Unified Vision Language Model

problem: Raw training loss is a biased and unstable proxy for difficulty, and scorer outputs are not directly comparable across samples.
method: Define difficulty as the negative of learned per-sample weight from the scorer (not raw loss), and apply inter-batch softmax normalization to make weights comparable. This stabilizes ranking and improves selection quality, enabling strong results with ~15% of data.
paper_title: Your Vision-Language Model Itself Is a Strong Filter: Towards High-Quality Instruction Tuning with Data Selection

problem: VLLM instruction fine-tuning erodes the underlying LLM's safety alignment, making VLLMs more vulnerable than base LLMs to text-only and multimodal jailbreaks; the root causes were unclear.
method: A systematic diagnosis: compare base LLMs vs their VLLMs on AdvBench (vanilla/suffix) and XSTest, audit popular VL instruction-tuning corpora with Llama-Guard to quantify harmful samples, and ablate fine-tuning regimes (LoRA vs full) and data cleaning. Findings show harmful data contamination and alignment forgetting, with LoRA more prone to safety degradation and data cleaning only partially restoring safety.
paper_title: Safety Fine-Tuning at (Almost) No Cost: A Baseline for Vision Large Language Models

problem: There is no public, multimodal safety dataset to align and evaluate VLLMs across visual and textual harm sources.
method: VLGuard: a curated vision-language safety instruction-following dataset built with GPT-4V over diverse image sources spanning 4 harm categories and 9 subcategories. For safe images, generate both safe and unsafe instruction–response pairs; for unsafe images, generate refusals with explanations. Provide a test suite split into Safe-Safe (helpfulness), Safe-Unsafe (language-side safety), and Unsafe (vision-side safety).
paper_title: Safety Fine-Tuning at (Almost) No Cost: A Baseline for Vision Large Language Models

problem: Practitioners need a low-cost, plug-and-play way to safety-align existing and newly trained VLLMs without sacrificing helpfulness.
method: Two fine-tuning strategies using VLGuard: (1) post-hoc safety fine-tuning augmented with a small amount of generic helpfulness data to avoid exaggerated refusals; (2) mixed fine-tuning by appending VLGuard (only 0.1–0.3% of total data) to standard training. Works with both full FT and LoRA, drives attack success near zero on AdvBench, XSTest, FigStep, and VLGuard while maintaining or improving helpfulness, increases robustness to black-box TAP and generalizes to unseen harm types, and trains in under an hour for 7B models on 2×A100 GPUs.
paper_title: Safety Fine-Tuning at (Almost) No Cost: A Baseline for Vision Large Language Models

problem: Zero-shot GPT-based VLN agents only reason over local observations and, when visual inputs are converted to text, lose crucial spatial context—leading to aimless exploration and weak global planning.
method: Map-guided prompting: build an online topological map during navigation and encode it as linguistic prompts (node IDs, explored/accessible/inaccessible nodes, and their connectivity), plus trajectory and annotations; discard metric/GPS coordinates to match LLM comprehension. This map-text is injected into GPT-4/GPT-4V inputs to provide a global view that guides exploration.
paper_title: MapGPT: Map-Guided Prompting with Adaptive Path Planning for Vision-and-Language Navigation

problem: Existing VLN agents rely on multi-expert, multi-stage text-only pipelines with high token cost and limited adaptability to multimodal LLMs.
method: A single-expert navigation prompt system that feeds instruction, history, directional action space, and observations directly to one LLM. It supports one-stage GPT-4V (images) or two-stage GPT-4 (captions/detections), reduces token usage, and seamlessly adapts across instruction styles (R2R/REVERIE).
paper_title: MapGPT: Map-Guided Prompting with Adaptive Path Planning for Vision-and-Language Navigation

problem: LLM agents lack robust long-horizon planning and error correction, typically choosing actions greedily without structured plans or backtracking.
method: Adaptive multi-step path planning: at each step GPT composes and iteratively updates a plan over the topological map, explicitly enumerating candidate nodes/sub-goals and scheduling exploration/backtracking (leveraging stored inaccessible nodes). This activates global reasoning and systematic exploration, improving OSR/SR via effective revisits and corrections.
paper_title: MapGPT: Map-Guided Prompting with Adaptive Path Planning for Vision-and-Language Navigation

problem: Estimating 3D human–object contact from a single in-the-wild image is hard and data-starved, as foundation models reason in 2D and 3D contact labels are scarce.
method: Render-Localize-Lift (RLL): render canonical human (SMPL+H) and retrieved object shapes from multiple views; a VLM (LLaVA) outputs contact reasoning tokens (<HCON>, <OCON>) whose embeddings are projected and lifted with a camera-aware FeatLift to become 3D-aware; a new SAM-based Multi-View Localization (MV-Loc) predicts per-view contact masks with a 3D consistency loss, then lifts them back to 3D, yielding accurate contacts with minimal 3D supervision.
paper_title: InteractVLM: 3D Interaction Reasoning from 2D Foundational Models

problem: Binary contact prediction cannot tell which object is being touched in multi-object scenes, limiting interaction understanding.
method: Introduce Semantic Human Contact: condition body-contact prediction on object semantics via language prompts. Extend the VLM vocabulary with contact tokens, fine-tune via LoRA using limited 3D contact data plus textual part labels and HOI/VQA descriptions, enabling object-conditioned, fine-grained body contact estimation that scales to many categories.
paper_title: InteractVLM: 3D Interaction Reasoning from 2D Foundational Models

problem: Joint 3D human–object reconstruction from a single image is ill-posed due to depth/occlusions and weak coupling between meshes.
method: Use InteractVLM’s predicted 3D contacts on both human and object to anchor reconstruction: retrieve an object mesh via OpenShape, initialize object pose by ICP aligning object/body contact points with normal consistency, then optimize pose/scale by differentiable render-and-compare with mask and contact losses, yielding more realistic HOI reconstructions in the wild.
paper_title: InteractVLM: 3D Interaction Reasoning from 2D Foundational Models

problem: Open-vocabulary dense segmentation benchmarks overlook imperceptible, camouflaged objects and contain semantic label ambiguities, hindering evaluation and progress.
method: Define the OVCOS task and curate the OVCamo dataset: 11,483 images with fine masks across 75 carefully re-labeled classes (reduced ambiguity and low inter-class similarity), attribute analyses (size, color similarity, shape complexity), and a class-disjoint split (14 seen/61 unseen) with adapted metrics for joint class+mask evaluation.
paper_title: Open-Vocabulary Camouflaged Object Segmentation

problem: CLIP-driven open-vocabulary segmentation struggles to localize camouflaged objects due to weak text–image alignment at pixel level and missing structural cues.
method: OVCoser, a single-stage transformer baseline attached to frozen CLIP, introduces (i) Semantic Guidance Attention that injects normalized class embeddings into every decoding stage; (ii) Structure Enhancement via auxiliary depth and edge branches supervising low-level layers and fused with learnable weights; and (iii) Iterative top-down refinement using visual–text correlation and object-aware pooled features to reweight attention. Depth/edge are training-only, keeping inference lightweight while markedly improving camo segmentation.
paper_title: Open-Vocabulary Camouflaged Object Segmentation

problem: Generic CLIP prompts misalign class semantics with camouflaged scenes, reducing open-vocabulary transfer.
method: CamoPrompts, a camouflage-aware prompt set (e.g., “camouflaged/concealed … blend in with surroundings”) with ensembling to form class embeddings, which reduces train–test embedding distance and improves classification/segmentation on OVCOS without tuning CLIP.
paper_title: Open-Vocabulary Camouflaged Object Segmentation

problem: Exhaustively probing large pretrained models (classification, summarization, text-to-image) to uncover accuracy errors, social biases, and misalignment across combinatorial ‘concept’ conditions is infeasible and stochastic.
method: Pose failure discovery as a deep RL problem over an actionable concept space wrapped around frozen models; train a DQN with an exploration schedule and two strategies—macroscopic (sporadic, wide exploration over concept combinations) and microscopic (incremental local edits from chosen states)—using task-tailored rewards that increase with failure likelihood/severity and (for microscopic) penalize steps, enabling efficient search in large discrete/continuous/hybrid spaces.
paper_title: Failures Are Fated, But Can Be Faded: Characterizing and Mitigating Unwanted Behaviors in Large-Scale Vision and Language Models

problem: Engineers and auditors need a compact, interpretable summary of high-dimensional failure landscapes and a way to inject limited human preferences to prioritize which failures matter.
method: Transform Q-values into action-selection probabilities to visualize failure regions and epistemic confidence; provide qualitative 3D summaries of top actions and quantitative aggregation via Wasserstein barycenters for high-D regions; gather a few post-hoc human ratings to select failures of interest, with an optional CLIP-based proxy reward that empirically aligns with human judgments.
paper_title: Failures Are Fated, But Can Be Faded: Characterizing and Mitigating Unwanted Behaviors in Large-Scale Vision and Language Models

problem: After identifying undesirable failure modes, practitioners need a post-hoc way to mitigate them without retraining from scratch or broadly degrading performance.
method: ‘Restructure’ the landscape via targeted fine-tuning guided by selected failure actions: last-layer tuning for classifiers, standard fine-tuning for BART/T5, and LoRA for Stable Diffusion; demonstrate shifts away from peak failure regions, reduced gender-bias ratios, and improved text/image quality, with evidence that CLIP-based rewards can substitute limited human feedback to learn similar mitigation policies.
paper_title: Failures Are Fated, But Can Be Faded: Characterizing and Mitigating Unwanted Behaviors in Large-Scale Vision and Language Models

problem: VLMs and image/video captioners miss subtle frame-to-frame differences and often hallucinate temporal progress, failing to produce frame-specific, temporally fine-grained captions.
method: ProgressCaptioner: a two-stage VLM tuned for progress-aware captioning—Stage I learns on adjacent frame pairs to capture nuanced changes; Stage II extends to multi-frame sequences via a two-frame sliding window. Training combines supervised fine-tuning with hallucination-aware direct preference optimization, using automatically constructed positive/negative caption pairs to enforce temporal specificity and reduce hallucination.
paper_title: Progress-Aware Video Frame Captioning

problem: Lack of scalable training data and reliable evaluation for frame-level, progress-aware video captioning.
method: FrameCap data pipeline and FrameCapEval benchmark: generate pseudo frame-wise captions from a VLM ensemble, then filter via two automatic critics—progression detection (LLM judges if visual progress exists) and caption matching (VLM multi-choice aligns captions to frames). Use accepted/rejected samples for SFT and preference learning; evaluate with sequence-level caption matching and balanced progression detection on curated videos from multiple datasets.
paper_title: Progress-Aware Video Frame Captioning

problem: Downstream tasks like keyframe selection and fine-grained video understanding need temporally precise signals but current methods rely on coarse event summaries or uniform sampling.
method: Leverage ProgressCaptioner’s per-frame progress-aware captions as a compact temporal representation: LLM-driven keyframe selection that adapts to actual action progress, and plug-in textual guidance to improve zero-shot frame-wise classification and video QA, outperforming uniform sampling and prior zero-shot baselines.
paper_title: Progress-Aware Video Frame Captioning
