problem: LVLMs conflate language priors with visual evidence, causing language hallucination (answering without/against visual input) and visual illusion (misreading visuals), and existing benchmarks cannot systematically disentangle or quantify these failures.
method: HALLUSIONBENCH, a human-crafted benchmark of 346 images (165 original, 181 human-edited) and 1129 yes/no questions organized into Visual Dependent vs Visual Supplement categories with control-pair design (original vs edited or with/without image). Diverse edits (flip, order reverse, OCR/object/color edits) and videos stress image-context reasoning, enabling disentanglement of hallucination vs illusion and robust measurement beyond simple accuracy.
paper_title: HALLUSIONBENCH: An Advanced Diagnostic Suite for Entangled Language Hallucination and Visual Illusion in Large Vision-Language Models

problem: Current LVLM evaluations emphasize raw accuracy or object hallucination and lack quantitative diagnostics of yes/no bias, logical consistency, and scalable, reliable judging.
method: A comprehensive evaluation suite: (1) new metrics—Question Pair Accuracy, Figure (consistency) Accuracy, Yes Percentage Difference and False Positive Ratio for yes/no bias; (2) a decision-tree diagnostic attributing errors to language hallucination, visual illusion, or mixed via control pairs; and (3) a text-only GPT-4-assisted judge (triplicate runs) aligned with human labels, with category-aware handling of 'uncertain' answers in no-visual supplement cases.
paper_title: HALLUSIONBENCH: An Advanced Diagnostic Suite for Entangled Language Hallucination and Visual Illusion in Large Vision-Language Models

problem: Normalization layers (e.g., LayerNorm/RMSNorm) are widely considered indispensable for stable and high-performing Transformer training, but they add computation, reduce simplicity, and require statistic reductions.
method: Introduce Dynamic Tanh (DyT): an element-wise, stat-free replacement for normalization defined as y = γ · tanh(αx) + β with a learnable global scalar α and per-channel γ, β. DyT preserves LN’s key effect—squashing extreme activations via a bounded S-shaped nonlinearity—while keeping the central region near-linear. It is a drop-in substitute in attention/FFN/final norm, trains stably with little/no hyperparameter tuning, and matches or exceeds LN/RMSNorm across vision classification/self-supervision, diffusion image generation, speech pretraining, DNA sequence modeling, and LLM pretraining.
paper_title: Transformers without Normalization

problem: The functional role of LayerNorm in deep Transformers is poorly understood, hindering principled normalization-free designs.
method: Empirically characterize LN’s input–output mapping across ViT, wav2vec 2.0, and DiT layers, showing an aggregate S-shaped, tanh-like curve: token-wise linear transforms with varying slopes compose into a global nonlinearity that disproportionately squashes extreme channels/tokens. This insight motivates DyT’s tanh core. Further analysis shows DyT’s α tracks ≈1/std of activations during and after training, explaining how DyT adapts global scale while tanh provides the necessary nonlinear squashing.
paper_title: Transformers without Normalization

problem: Existing normalization-free approaches (e.g., Fixup, SkipInit, σ-Reparam) often underperform or need careful tuning, especially at scale (e.g., large LLMs).
method: Extensive head-to-head experiments show DyT outperforms or matches LN/RMSNorm and surpasses other no-norm baselines on ImageNet (supervised, MAE, DINO), DiT (FID), wav2vec 2.0, DNA tasks, and LLaMA 7B–70B. Ablations confirm both the squashing function and learnable α are critical. For LLMs, a simple, width-aware α0 scheme with higher α0 in attention blocks and lower elsewhere stabilizes training and achieves parity with RMSNorm without altering recipes.
paper_title: Transformers without Normalization

problem: Existing document parsing benchmarks lack diversity (few page types, languages) and fine-grained, realistic annotations, preventing fair evaluation of pipelines and VLMs.
method: OmniDocBench: a high-quality dataset of 981 PDF pages spanning 9 document types (e.g., papers, textbooks, notes, newspapers) with comprehensive labels—19 layout categories, reading order, affiliations, and 15 attribute tags—plus recognition annotations (plain text, LaTeX formulas, tables in HTML+LaTeX). Data assembled via large-scale PDF collection, ResNet-50 + Faiss clustering, semi-automatic pre-annotation (LayoutLMv3, PaddleOCR, UniMERNet, GPT-4o), annotator correction, and expert QA.
paper_title: OmniDocBench: Benchmarking Diverse PDF Document Parsing with Comprehensive Annotations

problem: Current end-to-end evaluations are inconsistent and unfair across content types (text, tables, formulas) and formats, obscuring true model capability.
method: A unified, multi-level evaluation framework (end-to-end, task-specific, attribute-based) with standardized extraction and preprocessing, Adjacency Search Match to robustly align GT/pred paragraphs, ignore-handling for inconsistent components (headers/footers/captions), and content-appropriate metrics: Normalized Edit Distance for text, TEDS for tables, CDM/BLEU/Edit Distance for formulas, and Edit Distance for reading order.
paper_title: OmniDocBench: Benchmarking Diverse PDF Document Parsing with Comprehensive Annotations

problem: The field lacks a systematic comparison of pipeline tools, expert VLMs, and general VLMs across realistic page types and degradations to reveal strengths/weaknesses and bottlenecks.
method: Comprehensive benchmarking of MinerU/Marker/Mathpix (pipelines), Nougat/GOT-OCR (expert VLMs), and GPT-4o/Qwen2-VL/InternVL2 (general VLMs) across languages (EN/ZH), page types, layouts, and degradations (fuzzy, watermark, colored backgrounds). Findings: pipelines excel on dense/structured docs (e.g., newspapers) via layout segmentation; general VLMs generalize better to uncommon formats and are more robust to degradations; multi-column/complex layouts reduce accuracy broadly—highlighting the need for layout-aware, high-resolution VLM designs.
paper_title: OmniDocBench: Benchmarking Diverse PDF Document Parsing with Comprehensive Annotations

problem: Point-cloud 3D detectors based on projection or dense voxel grids lose geometric relations or are computationally heavy, while PointNet-style backbones underuse local neighbor structure and global context.
method: Voxel-graph module with spherical voxelization: sample centers by farthest point sampling and group points within a fixed-radius sphere; build a local complete graph inside each sphere and a global KNN graph across voxel centers. Apply attention on both graphs (softmax-normalized local attention and global KNN attention) and stack point-attention layers to jointly aggregate local and global features, then pool to per-voxel descriptors for detection.
paper_title: SVGA-Net: Sparse Voxel-Graph Attention Network for 3D Object Detection from Point Clouds

problem: Accurate 3D box regression needs multi-scale features that bridge sparse high-level context and dense low-level details without resorting to heavy 3D convolutions.
method: Sparse-to-Dense Regression (SDR): a three-stage feature hierarchy with downsampling; upsample and concatenate higher-level maps with lower-level ones, then parallel conv and upsampling to form aligned multi-scale maps, followed by element-wise fusion with original features to produce a final fused map for classification and 3D box regression, improving localization accuracy.
paper_title: SVGA-Net: Sparse Voxel-Graph Attention Network for 3D Object Detection from Point Clouds

problem: No unified, low-cost way to predict how camera-LiDAR placements and parameters affect 3D detection performance without exhaustive data collection and training.
method: An information-theoretic surrogate metric that models both sensors via a unified ray-casting sensing model (camera pixels downsampled to rays; LiDAR beams as rotating cones), builds a probabilistic occupancy grid (POG) over ROI, and computes per-sensor information gain (S-MIG) as the mutual information between full and conditional voxel entropies; extends to a unified multi-sensor score (S-MS) via a weighted sum (lambda) to balance camera/LiDAR capabilities.
paper_title: Influence of Camera-LiDAR Configuration on 3D Object Detection for Autonomous Driving

problem: Evaluating many sensor configurations in the real world is slow and costly, hindering design-space exploration and validation of metrics.
method: An accelerated CARLA-based pipeline that automatically generates nuScenes-like datasets for multiple camera/LiDAR layouts, trains representative fusion detectors (TransFusion, BEVFusion, PointAugmenting), and correlates mAP with the surrogate metric, showing configuration-driven performance swings up to ~30% and validating the metric’s predictive power.
paper_title: Influence of Camera-LiDAR Configuration on 3D Object Detection for Autonomous Driving

problem: Recovering fine textures in x16 perceptual single-image super-resolution while keeping computation manageable; conventional multi-scale designs with large kernels are costly and miss detailed line features.
method: Integrate multi-scale Receptive Field Blocks (RFB) into an ESRGAN backbone by forming Residual of Receptive Field Dense Blocks (RRFDBs). RFBs use multi-branch dilated paths with small/asymmetric kernels (1x1, 1x3, 3x1) instead of large kernels, drop BN, and use LeakyReLU to capture rich multi-scale, fine-grained details at reduced complexity.
paper_title: Perceptual Extreme Super Resolution Network with Receptive Field Block

problem: A single upsampling method (nearest-neighbor or sub-pixel) either blurs textures or introduces artifacts and weak spatial–channel information exchange, while upsampling is a major compute bottleneck.
method: Alternate nearest-neighbor interpolation and sub-pixel convolution in the upsampling stage, placing an RFB after each step to propagate transformations between space and depth. This improves perceptual sharpness and naturalness while reducing parameters/time.
paper_title: Perceptual Extreme Super Resolution Network with Receptive Field Block

problem: GAN-based SR outputs can be noisy and unstable across diverse images; typical fusions mix PSNR- and GAN-oriented models, diluting perceptual quality.
method: Ensemble by parameter-averaging the top 10 GAN-only checkpoints (no PSNR-model mixing) to suppress noise and improve robustness; empirically selects 10 as the best trade-off for perceptual metrics.
paper_title: Perceptual Extreme Super Resolution Network with Receptive Field Block

problem: One-shot keypoint extraction struggles to generalize across large domain shifts and viewpoint changes due to limited task-specific data and weak local structure in ViT features.
method: Leverage off-the-shelf DINO ViT features augmented by training-free feature enhancement: (i) objectness attention from mean absolute activation with sigmoid scaling to suppress background, and (ii) neighborhood binning that concatenates local and pooled neighboring features to encode 2D local structure—improving recall/precision without any finetuning.
paper_title: AnyOKP: One-Shot and Instance-Aware Object Keypoint Extraction with Pretrained ViT

problem: Conventional one-shot correspondence assumes one-to-one matches, failing to detect keypoints on multiple instances of the same category in a query image.
method: Best-Prototype Pair (BPP) extraction: learn keypoint prototypes from a single support image, then select all query pixels whose best match is a given prototype (including adjacent support pixels) and apply weak-threshold NMS—enabling one-to-many correspondences to produce instance-unaware candidate keypoints across multiple object instances.
paper_title: AnyOKP: One-Shot and Instance-Aware Object Keypoint Extraction with Pretrained ViT

problem: Candidate keypoints from one-to-many matching are instance-unaware and can mix across different object instances or include false positives.
method: Edge-based keypoint grouping: learn edge prototypes on the support graph by averaging ViT features along subdivided keypoint-to-keypoint edges; in the query, compute edge similarities and prune edges via thresholding and competitive pruning among edges sharing a vertex, partitioning the graph into subgraphs that each correspond to an object instance.
paper_title: AnyOKP: One-Shot and Instance-Aware Object Keypoint Extraction with Pretrained ViT

problem: Selecting an ensemble of pre-trained source models that will transfer best to a target task without exhaustively fine-tuning all combinations; prior multi-source TE largely ignores domain (feature) mismatch and inter-model interactions.
method: OSBORN: a transferability metric combining (i) domain difference via 1-Wasserstein Optimal Transport between source and target latent features (Sinkhorn), (ii) task difference via conditional entropy of source-predicted labels on target conditioned on target labels (joint estimated from OT coupling), and (iii) inter-model cohesiveness via conditional entropy capturing disagreement among models’ predictions; lower combined score indicates better transferability; domain/task-agnostic and label-space-agnostic.
paper_title: Building a Winning Team: Selecting Source Model Ensembles using a Submodular Transferability Estimation Approach

problem: Finding the best ensemble is a combinatorial, NP-hard selection problem; need an efficient selector with guarantees.
method: Formulate the OSBORN-based set function as a submodular, monotone objective and apply greedy maximization under a cardinality constraint to rank and pick k models with a 1−1/e approximation guarantee; precompute pairwise W_D and W_T and update W_C incrementally, enabling scalable, parallelizable selection that achieves near-optimal accuracy in practice.
paper_title: Building a Winning Team: Selecting Source Model Ensembles using a Submodular Transferability Estimation Approach

problem: Transferability estimators often fail to generalize across tasks (classification, segmentation, domain adaptation) and pretraining regimes (supervised vs. self-supervised).
method: Extensive benchmarking across 28 source datasets, 11 targets, 5 architectures, and both supervised and SSL pretraining; OSBORN consistently yields higher PCC/KT/WKT correlations and better selected-ensemble accuracies than MS-LEEP/E-LEEP; ablations validate the cohesiveness term and show a Frobenius-regularized OT variant particularly benefits SSL settings.
paper_title: Building a Winning Team: Selecting Source Model Ensembles using a Submodular Transferability Estimation Approach

problem: Monocular 3D detectors that rely on sparse 2D cues (edges/keypoints) provide indirect and brittle supervision for depth, lacking dense pixel-level 2D–3D correspondences.
method: Predict a per-pixel normalized object coordinate (scale-invariant NOCS) map within each ROI, trained with LiDAR-supervised L1 loss where available and unsupervised reprojection consistency otherwise, to establish dense 2D–3D matches for precise depth reasoning.
paper_title: MonoJSG: Joint Semantic and Geometric Cost Volume for Monocular 3D Object Detection

problem: Geometric-only or raw photometric alignment is unreliable in textureless/occluded regions and leads to non-convex objectives that hinder accurate depth refinement.
method: Build a 4D joint semantic-and-geometric cost volume per proposal by reprojecting dense object coordinates at candidate depths and concatenating pixel coordinates (p, p̂) with learned semantic features (F(p), F(p̂)); pass it through a lightweight 2D/3D-conv refinement network with soft arg-margin depth selection, using direct depth supervision to learn discriminative features and regularize the depth error distribution.
paper_title: MonoJSG: Joint Semantic and Geometric Cost Volume for Monocular 3D Object Detection

problem: Instance-specific depth error distributions make fixed uniform depth sampling either miss the true depth or waste computation/memory.
method: Adopt uncertainty-guided adaptive sampling for the cost volume by setting the depth grid size from the proposal’s predicted Laplacian uncertainty (∆z = λ·σz) with a fixed number of candidates and coordinate normalization, balancing coverage with real-time efficiency.
paper_title: MonoJSG: Joint Semantic and Geometric Cost Volume for Monocular 3D Object Detection

problem: Long-range LiDAR 3D detection is computationally expensive for voxel/sparse-conv methods and range-image-only methods struggle with occlusion and precise localization.
method: Range Sparse Net (RSN) uses a lightweight 2D U-Net on high-resolution range images to perform high-recall foreground segmentation and learn semantic features; only selected foreground points are dynamically voxelized, enriched with range-image features, and processed by 2D/3D sparse convolutions. This multi-view fusion preserves range resolution for small/distant objects and gates sparse convs to a tiny subset of points, boosting accuracy and efficiency.
paper_title: RSN: Range Sparse Net for Efficient, Accurate LiDAR 3D Object Detection

problem: Standard detection heads require dense processing and NMS, adding latency and complexity on sparse voxel features.
method: RSN adapts CenterNet to sparse voxels: it predicts a centerness heatmap over sparse voxel coordinates and regresses 3D boxes with smooth L1 + IoU + heading-bin losses; sparse submanifold max pooling selects local maxima, removing NMS and enabling fast end-to-end box prediction on sparse maps.
paper_title: RSN: Range Sparse Net for Efficient, Accurate LiDAR 3D Object Detection

problem: Temporal fusion of LiDAR range images is hard due to ego-motion and quantization when stacking frames, often increasing cost without gains.
method: Temporal RSN processes each frame’s range image with shared RIFE to segment and extract features, transforms selected foreground points to the latest frame to remove ego-motion, appends per-frame voxel statistics and time deltas, then fuses for SPFE; during inference it reuses cached past RIFE outputs, improving accuracy with near single-frame latency.
paper_title: RSN: Range Sparse Net for Efficient, Accurate LiDAR 3D Object Detection

problem: Instance segmentation under partial occlusion degrades because objects are modeled independently and dense mask annotations are costly.
method: Extend CompositionalNets to a multi-object generative model over neural features with an explicit outlier (occluder) model, producing per-object foreground/context/occlusion likelihood maps and instance masks from bounding-box supervision only; use top-down refinement by masking occluded features to improve classification.
paper_title: Robust Instance Segmentation through Reasoning about Multi-Object Occlusion

problem: Independent per-object predictions create inconsistent masks in overlapping regions and make multi-object occlusion inference intractable.
method: Introduce an Occlusion Reasoning Module that detects pixel-level conflicts via occlusion voting, resolves them through pixel-level competition on foreground likelihoods, recovers pairwise occlusion order (using votes/area and classification scores), and enforces all-or-nothing assignment in overlaps; iteratively feed corrected masks back to self-correct recognition and segmentation.
paper_title: Robust Instance Segmentation through Reasoning about Multi-Object Occlusion

problem: Evaluating robustness to complex occlusions lacks controlled datasets with accurate amodal ground truth and unknown occluders.
method: Build a synthetic occlusion challenge by compositing segmented KITTI objects onto backgrounds to generate precise amodal masks and occlusion order, spanning scenarios: pairwise occlusion, 4-object mutual occlusion, and mixed known/unknown occluders.
paper_title: Robust Instance Segmentation through Reasoning about Multi-Object Occlusion

problem: Many self-supervised pretext tasks (e.g., rotation, jigsaw) learn features that covary with image transformations, which is misaligned with the invariance needed for semantic recognition.
method: Pretext-Invariant Representation Learning (PIRL) formulates a contrastive objective that makes an image’s representation similar to representations of its transformed views and dissimilar to other images, using a noise-contrastive estimator; this shifts from predicting transformations to enforcing invariance and is applicable to any transformation-defined pretext task.
paper_title: Self-Supervised Learning of Pretext-Invariant Representations

problem: Integrating complex transformations like Jigsaw into an invariance framework without output-layer explosion and while remaining comparable to covariant baselines.
method: Use two heads: f on the original image’s global pooled features and g on the transformed image’s patchwise features (nine ResNet-5 patch embeddings projected, concatenated in random order, then projected), and align them via the contrastive loss. This design preserves Jigsaw structure, enables using all 9! permutations without growing parameters, and encourages transformation-invariant semantics.
paper_title: Self-Supervised Learning of Pretext-Invariant Representations

problem: Contrastive learning requires many negatives and stable targets, and standard NCE may not compare untransformed images or stabilize updates sufficiently.
method: Introduce a memory bank of moving-average features for all images to supply large-scale negatives and define a convex combination of two NCE terms (controlled by λ): one aligning I with its transformed I_t against negatives, and another aligning current features to their memory while repelling other images. This generalizes NPID (λ=0) and improves invariance and training stability.
paper_title: Self-Supervised Learning of Pretext-Invariant Representations

problem: Vanilla/global self-attention in ViTs is quadratically expensive and binds Q/K/V dimensions to the input, limiting context-oriented generalization and yielding discontinuous, object-insensitive features.
method: Scalable Self-Attention (SSA) introduces two learnable scaling factors on Q/K/V: spatial scaling (reduce token count by rn) to aggregate redundant tokens and channel scaling (expand/compress channels by rc) to enrich representation, implemented via conv + linear projections and restoring output dims for residuals. This decouples Q/K/V from input size, reduces complexity to linear in N, and yields more continuous, context-aware object activations.
paper_title: ScalableViT: Rethinking the Context-oriented Generalization of Vision Transformer

problem: Window-based self-attention (WSA) loses cross-window interactions, causing isolated activations and weak long-range dependencies within a single layer.
method: Interactive Window-based Self-Attention (IWSA) augments WSA with a Local Interactive Module (LIM): re-merge per-window value tokens into a global map and apply a lightweight depth-wise convolution (e.g., 3x3 with zero padding) in parallel to attention to propagate information across adjacent windows. This restores a single-layer global receptive field with negligible overhead and improves downstream detection/segmentation.
paper_title: ScalableViT: Rethinking the Context-oriented Generalization of Vision Transformer

problem: Diffusion-based perception models (for segmentation, depth, detection) underperform when conditioned on unaligned or generic prompts, which corrupt cross-attention and downstream features.
method: Text-Aligned Diffusion Perception (TADP): automatically generate image-specific prompts with a captioner (BLIP-2) and feed them to a Stable Diffusion backbone; concatenate cross-attention maps and multi-scale features for a task head, and normalize image latents via latent scaling. Longer, noun-rich captions further help. This improves SOTA on ADE20K segmentation and NYUv2 depth.
paper_title: Text-image Alignment for Diffusion-based Perception

problem: Existing prompting practice for diffusion backbones uses averaged EOS class embeddings, but its effect on attention and perception quality is unclear and often harmful.
method: A systematic prompting study replacing averaged EOS embeddings with actual class-name tokens or class-name lists, analyzing cross-attention and performance. Findings: EOS-averaging yields diffuse attention and worse accuracy; off-target/missing classes degrade results; nouns-only captions retain most gains; higher recall of present classes is critical. Provides practical prompt design rules for diffusion-based perception.
paper_title: Text-image Alignment for Diffusion-based Perception

problem: Cross-domain transfer without target labels suffers from large domain shifts, limiting source-trained diffusion-based perception models.
method: Text–target-domain alignment: train on source images with captions modified to encode target style (hand-crafted descriptors) and personalize the diffusion model using Textual Inversion or DreamBooth to learn domain-specific tokens from unlabeled target images; at test time, clean captions to control style leakage. This yields SOTA/near-SOTA on VOC→Watercolor2K/Comic2K detection and Cityscapes→Dark Zurich/Nighttime segmentation.
paper_title: Text-image Alignment for Diffusion-based Perception

problem: Lightweight on-device detectors degrade and catastrophically forget under continual distribution shifts, but devices cannot frequently retrain due to compute and bandwidth limits.
method: Cloud-Device Collaborative Continual Adaptation with a U-VPA teacher–student framework: a large cloud teacher is optimized with adversarial feature alignment on source/target and generates high-quality pseudo-labels; a lightweight student learns from these labels. A novel pixel-level Visual Prompt Adaptation (VPA) is learned to concatenate prompts to inputs, pulling device test distribution toward the cloud’s training distribution. Prompts are updated via uncertainty-aware EMA and, together with the updated student, are transmitted back to the device, providing bandwidth-efficient continual adaptation.
paper_title: Cloud-Device Collaborative Adaptation to Continual Changing Environments in the Real-world

problem: Under tight uplink bandwidth, sending all test frames to the cloud for adaptation is infeasible, especially when environments change continually.
method: Uncertainty Guided Sampling (UGS) on-device: use MC-dropout to compute prediction variance per image for object detection and transmit only high-uncertainty (environment-specific) samples to the cloud. This focuses adaptation on OOD data, reduces uplink traffic, and improves performance compared to sending full or randomly sampled data.
paper_title: Cloud-Device Collaborative Adaptation to Continual Changing Environments in the Real-world

problem: Designing efficient adversarial attacks that maximally increase object detector inference latency on edge devices, while avoiding the heavy compute/memory of prior NMS-targeted methods.
method: Analyze NMS time complexity to show latency is dominated by the number of candidates fed to NMS (|C|) and is largely independent of surviving boxes. Formulate a simple white-box PGD objective that monotonically increases objectness and class probabilities for all anchors to inflate |C|, avoiding pairwise IoU/area losses and thus reducing compute and memory; this yields an NMS-agnostic latency attack (Overload).
paper_title: Overload: Latency Attacks on Object Detection for Edge Devices

problem: Standard PGD-style attacks tend to overpopulate already dense areas, limiting the total number of activated boxes and thus the achievable latency increase.
method: Introduce spatial attention: tile the image into grids and adaptively reweight gradients to downweight regions already producing many objects and upweight sparse regions. Iteratively steer perturbations to low-density tiles, driving the detector toward near-maximum object activations and larger latency increases without added IoU computations.
paper_title: Overload: Latency Attacks on Object Detection for Edge Devices

problem: Establishing the universality and real-world impact of latency attacks across NMS implementations and resource-constrained hardware.
method: Provide IoU-invariant verification and synthetic worst/best-case analyses showing latency scales primarily with |C| across IoU thresholds and NMS variants. Deploy Overload on TensorRT-compiled YOLOv5s/n on Jetson NX and multiple GPUs, demonstrating 3–10× slowdowns and pipeline-level FPS collapse, highlighting NMS-agnostic, hardware-relevant DoS risk.
paper_title: Overload: Latency Attacks on Object Detection for Edge Devices

problem: Visual Prompt Tuning (VPT) underperforms on self-supervised ViTs because prompt tokens are indiscriminately influenced by all blocks; the optimal blocks to steer vary across SSL pretraining (e.g., MAE, MoCo v3) and downstream tasks, making manual search for insertion locations costly.
method: Gated Prompt Tuning: prepend prompts once (as in VPT-shallow) and introduce a learnable scalar gate per ViT block that convexly mixes the prompt before/after each block, selectively allowing only task-relevant blocks to update the prompt. This yields sample-conditioned, task-relevant instructions without block-location search and improves accuracy/efficiency across FGVC, VTAB-1K, and ADE20K.
paper_title: Improving Visual Prompt Tuning for Self-supervised Vision Transformers

problem: Frozen self-attention in SSL ViTs may not align with target tasks, limiting how well prompts can steer representations.
method: Adaptive Attention Shaping: equip each block with a learnable temperature that rescales attention logits, dynamically sharpening or smoothing attention to better route information and enhance prompt effectiveness; a plug-in that consistently boosts transfer performance when combined with gating.
paper_title: Improving Visual Prompt Tuning for Self-supervised Vision Transformers

problem: Medical image segmentation requires precise boundary delineation, but existing SAM-based adaptations and standard fine-tuning overlook frequency-domain cues critical for boundaries.
method: Introduce a lightweight frequency adapter that extracts FFT amplitude features and injects them at each ViT block alongside LoRA-based PEFT, enriching SAM features with high-frequency information to sharpen boundaries.
paper_title: I-MedSAM: Implicit Medical Image Segmentation with Segment Anything

problem: Implicit decoders typically use random point sampling or dense prediction, which is inefficient and misses hard regions, leading to suboptimal refinement.
method: Design a coarse-to-fine implicit neural representation (two stacked INRs): a shallow ‘coarse’ INR produces initial masks and features; uncertainty is estimated via MC-Dropout variance, and Top-K uncertain coordinates are selected (UGS) for a deeper ‘fine’ INR to refine predictions.
paper_title: I-MedSAM: Implicit Medical Image Segmentation with Segment Anything

problem: Discrete pixel-wise decoders are spatially inflexible and scale poorly to high resolutions; existing implicit methods lack strong pretrained encoders and exhibit weak cross-domain generalization.
method: Combine SAM’s powerful image and prompt encoders (adapted with LoRA) with an implicit decoder that maps features and normalized coordinates (plus bbox prompts) to continuous segmentation at arbitrary resolutions, achieving strong cross-resolution/domain performance with only ~1.6M trainable parameters.
paper_title: I-MedSAM: Implicit Medical Image Segmentation with Segment Anything

problem: Non-invasive, continuous monitoring of hive activity (arrivals/leavings and overall traffic) at the hive entrance is labor-intensive and error-prone, with occlusions and varying lighting degrading vision accuracy on low-cost hardware.
method: A cost-effective entrance-mounted vision system using a custom enclosure (laser-cut wood, fishing-line mesh to reduce glare/occlusion) and a YOLOv7-tiny detector trained on in-hive video for bee detection, paired with a lightweight centroid-based tracker with vertical trigger lines to robustly count arriving/leaving bees and estimate bee size; achieves 96.28% tracking accuracy in real-world conditions.
paper_title: IntelliBeeHive: An Automated Honey Bee, Pollen, and Varroa Destructor Monitoring System

problem: Automated assessment of foraging (pollen collection) and early warning of Varroa mite presence typically require invasive sampling or manual inspection.
method: A dual-stage edge pipeline: crop full-bee images at trigger crossings from the primary detector, then run a second YOLOv7-tiny specialized for ‘Pollen’ and ‘Mite’ detection on the crops to reduce compute and false detections; trained on custom pollen data (and simulated mites for feasibility), yielding an F1 of 0.831 for pollen on-field videos.
paper_title: IntelliBeeHive: An Automated Honey Bee, Pollen, and Varroa Destructor Monitoring System

problem: Near–real-time inference and data upload on a low-cost embedded platform (Jetson Nano) is constrained by compute and thermal limits, risking long delays between acquisition and insights.
method: Model and system-level optimization: convert YOLOv7-tiny to ONNX then TensorRT to halve per-frame latency (56 ms → 27 ms), downscale frames, and use an overlapped record-then-process pipeline (GStreamer + cron) to keep a ~5-minute delay; PoE-powered deployment streams measurements via a REST API to a web dashboard for scalable visualization.
paper_title: IntelliBeeHive: An Automated Honey Bee, Pollen, and Varroa Destructor Monitoring System

problem: Deterministic uncertainty methods often rely on bi-Lipschitz constraints that hinder scalability to complex/high-resolution vision tasks and suffer from feature collapse, hurting OOD detection.
method: Introduce LDU: insert a distinction maximization layer in the pre-logit latent space with an arbitrarily-sized set of trainable prototypes; use cosine similarity with exponential activation to shape a discriminative latent space that preserves sensitivity without enforcing explicit Lipschitz constraints.
paper_title: Latent Discriminant Deterministic Uncertainty

problem: Latent prototypes can collapse or fail to encode uncertainty, and single-pass models struggle to capture meaningful confidence signals (aleatoric/epistemic) without extra passes or auxiliary networks.
method: Couple the prototype layer with targeted regularization and an uncertainty head: (i) a prototype dissimilarity loss to spread prototypes, (ii) an entropy-like loss to encourage features to align with multiple prototypes, and (iii) an MLP trained to predict normalized task loss (via BCE) from prototype activations; optionally train on synthesized outliers to emphasize epistemic uncertainty.
paper_title: Latent Discriminant Deterministic Uncertainty

problem: Accurate and calibrated uncertainty across diverse CV tasks (classification, segmentation, depth) typically requires costly ensembles or test-time sampling.
method: Provide a plug-and-play, single-forward-pass layer that improves accuracy and delivers competitive OOD detection and calibration by leveraging the learned prototype-space geometry; validated on CIFAR-10/SVHN, MUAD, Cityscapes/Cityscapes-C, BDD Anomaly, and KITTI depth with runtime close to a baseline model.
paper_title: Latent Discriminant Deterministic Uncertainty

problem: Research on counting, localization, and tracking in drone-captured crowded videos is hindered by the absence of a large-scale, well-annotated benchmark with trajectories under challenging aerial conditions (tiny objects, scale/viewpoint changes, clutter).
method: Construct DroneCrowd: 112 HD video clips (33,600 frames) across 70 scenarios with 4.8M head annotations and 20,800 trajectories, plus video-level attributes (illumination, scale, density) and standardized protocols/metrics (MAE/MSE for counting, L-mAP for localization at pixel thresholds, T-mAP for tracking at match-ratio thresholds), with train/test split across locations.
paper_title: Detection, Tracking, and Counting Meets Drones in Crowds: A Benchmark

problem: Jointly estimating density (counting), localizing heads, and associating targets over time in dense drone videos is difficult; prior approaches often treat tasks separately and underuse temporal cues for tiny, crowded targets.
method: Propose STNNet (Space-Time Neighbor-Aware Network): a Siamese VGG-16 backbone with U-Net multiscale fusion and cross-frame correlation, density heads for counting, a localization subnet (per-pixel proposals with classification/regression and CBAM attention), and an association subnet that predicts motion offsets using PointConv over top-scored proposals and multi-scale correlation features; trained end-to-end with a multi-task loss.
paper_title: Detection, Tracking, and Counting Meets Drones in Crowds: A Benchmark

problem: Associating small, crowded targets suffers from occlusion and appearance ambiguity; standard losses don’t enforce motion coherence among nearby objects across frames.
method: Design a neighboring context loss that guides the association subnet via (i) a temporal prediction constraint and (ii) a relation (neighbor) constraint that penalizes changes in relative position vectors between adjacent objects, coupled with a cycle strategy across consecutive frames; this leverages spatial-temporal neighbor consistency to produce accurate motion offsets and improves localization/tracking.
paper_title: Detection, Tracking, and Counting Meets Drones in Crowds: A Benchmark

problem: Image-based KD transfers poorly to 3D point clouds, which lack regular topology and are irregular/sparse, so students fail to inherit teachers’ understanding of local geometric structure.
method: Local distillation of geometric structure: build KNN neighborhoods around top-N important voxels/points, encode relations with dynamic graph convolution, and distill these graph features from teacher to student; the graph layers are trained jointly and discarded at inference, making the approach architecture-agnostic and effective for both voxel- and point-based 3D detectors.
paper_title: PointDistiller: Structured Knowledge Distillation Towards Efficient and Compact 3D Detection

problem: Point cloud sparsity/noise means many voxels contain a single (often noisy) point, so uniformly distilling all locations wastes capacity and harms student learning.
method: Reweighted knowledge distillation with importance-aware sampling: compute per-voxel/point importance from teacher features (voxel: number of points; point-based: channel-wise max), sample the top-N to distill, and weight the KD loss via a softmax of importance (with temperature) to emphasize informative multi-point voxels/influential points, improving efficiency and accuracy under compression.
paper_title: PointDistiller: Structured Knowledge Distillation Towards Efficient and Compact 3D Detection

problem: Medical image segmentation models trained with scarce, pixel-level supervision learn noisy/bias-prone semantic features, hurting both in-domain accuracy and out-of-domain robustness.
method: Semantic Stacking (S2S2): estimate a denoised semantic representation by constructing a stack of images that share the same segmentation map and enforcing feature consistency with this pooled representation during training. Implemented as an auxiliary semantic consistency loss (e.g., 1 − cosine similarity) on encoder/decoder features, added to standard segmentation loss; domain-agnostic and plug-and-play across models and modalities.
paper_title: S2S2: Semantic Stacking for Robust Semantic Segmentation in Medical Imaging

problem: Naively learning from large semantic stacks is computationally impractical, requiring many forward passes and stored activations per iteration.
method: A theoretical analysis (Bayesian updating + triangle inequality) shows that minimizing the distance to the pooled denoised feature is upper-bounded by pairwise feature distances, enabling training with only two images per iteration. This yields a practical, scalable semantic consistency loss that approximates learning from the full stack.
paper_title: S2S2: Semantic Stacking for Robust Semantic Segmentation in Medical Imaging

problem: Obtaining multiple real images per segmentation map to form a semantic stack is infeasible in medical imaging due to data scarcity and limited diversity.
method: Construct semantic stacks via conditional generative synthesis: fine-tune Stable Diffusion with ControlNet conditioned on ground-truth masks to generate diverse images per mask. The generated features’ mean approximates the true semantic feature, providing a data-driven, domain-agnostic way to improve robustness across CT, MRI, and RGB without relying on domain-specific augmentations.
paper_title: S2S2: Semantic Stacking for Robust Semantic Segmentation in Medical Imaging

problem: On-device CNN training is bottlenecked by the computational and memory cost of backpropagating through convolution layers, which current techniques (e.g., gradient quantization) cannot significantly reduce or are unsupported in standard DL libraries.
method: Introduce gradient filtering: spatially partition the output gradient map into r×r patches and replace each patch with its mean to impose a low-complexity, structured gradient with few unique values; pre-sum inputs and rotated kernels over spatial dimensions to convert convolutional BP into cheap per-channel multiplications/additions, cutting FLOPs by ~1/(r^2·HθWθ) and activation memory by ~1−1/r^2, in a drop-in implementation for OneDNN/CUDNN.
paper_title: Efficient On-device Training via Gradient Filtering

problem: Approximate gradients can degrade learning; practical deployment needs guarantees that accuracy loss remains bounded.
method: Provide a frequency-domain error analysis proving that, under DC-dominant kernel spectra, the SNR of filtered gradients increases after convolution, bounding error propagation; derive computation/memory formulas and the minimum achievable computation to guide patch-size selection and quantify trade-offs.
paper_title: Efficient On-device Training via Gradient Filtering

problem: Edge deployment requires framework-compatible kernels that deliver real speed/energy savings across diverse CV models and tasks.
method: Implement the gradient filtering pipeline in MKLDNN/CUDNN and validate on ImageNet classification and semantic segmentation (MobileNet/ResNet/DeepLabV3/UPerNet) on Jetson/Raspberry Pi/desktop, achieving up to 19–229× speedup, ~77–95% memory/energy savings with marginal accuracy loss; demonstrate composability with INT8 gradient quantization for additional gains.
paper_title: Efficient On-device Training via Gradient Filtering

problem: Deploying multi-LLM agents is costly, and existing methods either minimize API cost without reliability guarantees or improve reliability without cost control; practitioners need to minimize expected cost while guaranteeing a user-specified reliability level.
method: Introduce CCPO, which unifies constrained policy optimization with online conformal prediction: parameterize a score-based policy π and an adaptive threshold κ to form a conformal policy that selects action sets, optimize π to minimize API cost under a coverage constraint, and update κ online to provably achieve target coverage (plus optional set-size penalty).
paper_title: Conformal Constrained Policy Optimization for Cost-Effective LLM Agents

problem: Optimizing set-valued (conformal) action policies is combinatorial and the behavior policy’s trajectory distribution differs from the conformal-wrapped target policy, causing unstable/off-policy learning.
method: Convert the set-valued conformal policy into a stochastic surrogate Sπ,κ that is uniform over the conformal action set and train it with V-trace importance-weighted off-policy critics and trust-region (CPO-style) policy updates; use a smooth sigmoid softmask to relax the indicator and clipped importance weights for stable gradients while approximately enforcing both cost and coverage objectives without enumerating action sets.
paper_title: Conformal Constrained Policy Optimization for Cost-Effective LLM Agents

problem: LLM agents lack a principled, low-cost orchestration scheme that leverages strong models’ judgment without paying their full generation cost while ensuring reliability.
method: Design a base–guide orchestration POMDP: a cheap base LLM proposes reasoning and answers; a strong guide LLM provides binary judgments and corrected answers using mostly input tokens; the policy chooses among accepting base, accepting guide, or continuing. Frame reliability as a conformal coverage constraint over the induced answer set (if any action sequence can reach the true answer, the set must contain it), enabling cost-effective, guaranteed-reliable orchestration.
paper_title: Conformal Constrained Policy Optimization for Cost-Effective LLM Agents

problem: RL for long-horizon visuomotor manipulation with sparse rewards fails due to poor exploration or requires many demos/shaped rewards.
method: ACGD adaptively sets task difficulty using reverse-trajectory sampling from a few demonstrations and on-policy success-rate feedback to keep performance within a target interval; it uses separate difficulty coefficients for demo vs. regular resets and a mixing policy to avoid demo bias.
paper_title: Adaptive Curriculum Generation from Demonstrations for Sim-to-Real Visuomotor Control

problem: Strong domain randomization is needed for sim-to-real transfer but harms learning if applied too early or too much.
method: Treat both appearance and dynamics randomization as curriculum variables and automatically scale them with the same success-rate-controlled difficulty coefficients, unifying start-state distance and environment parameter variance; handle demo-initialized and regular resets with distinct parameter sets.
paper_title: Adaptive Curriculum Generation from Demonstrations for Sim-to-Real Visuomotor Control

problem: Zero-shot transfer of vision-based robot policies without real-world finetuning.
method: Train PPO visuomotor policies in simulation under ACGD with gradually increased domain randomization, then deploy directly on real robots for pick-and-stow and block stacking, achieving high success and outperforming BC, shaped-reward RL, and non-adaptive/linear curricula.
paper_title: Adaptive Curriculum Generation from Demonstrations for Sim-to-Real Visuomotor Control

problem: Multi-object navigation methods typically require a pre-specified exploration order (PSM) or compute a global sequence, which is impractical and computationally expensive in dynamic homes/factories.
method: SAM: a modular deep RL actor-critic policy that, from an online allocentric semantic map built via RGB-D + Mask R-CNN and multi-goal encodings, learns to greedily output long-term goal regions that concurrently reduce distance to all targets, executed by a deterministic planner (Fast Marching). This extends prior ON architecture to multi-goal inputs without explicit sequence computation.
paper_title: Sequence-Agnostic Multi-Object Navigation

problem: Standard ON/MultiON rewards encourage progress toward a single goal and fail to incentivize concurrent progress and time efficiency, leading to myopic or procrastinating behavior.
method: A novel reward design combining (i) a sub-goal reward upon localizing any target, (ii) a process reward based on per-timestep decreases in geodesic distance to the nearest instance of each remaining target (using ground-truth distances during training), scaled to encourage simultaneous progress, and (iii) a small step penalty. Trained with an actor-critic where the critic uses clipped double Q-learning, yielding sequence-agnostic, non-procrastinating policies.
paper_title: Sequence-Agnostic Multi-Object Navigation

problem: Single RL agents overfit and fail to adapt to non-stationary, regime-shifting crypto markets; position information is underutilized in policy decisions.
method: Decompose market data by both trend and volatility into regimes and train specialized DDQN-dueling sub-agents per regime. Each sub-agent uses a conditional adapter that adapts the policy via adaptive layer-norm style scale/shift from market context features and discrete position embeddings, with an Optimal Value Supervisor (KL to Q*) to reduce overfitting.
paper_title: MacroHFT: Memory Augmented Context-aware Reinforcement Learning On High Frequency Trading

problem: Selecting a single agent at each step yields biased decisions and unstable performance under rapid market shifts and rare extreme events.
method: Train a hyper-agent that outputs softmax weights to mix sub-agents’ Q-values into a meta-policy at the same minute-level timescale, conditioned on recent trend/volatility. Augment it with a table-based episodic memory that retrieves nearest recent experiences and adds a memory-derived target to the Q-loss, stabilizing learning and enabling fast adaptation to abrupt fluctuations.
paper_title: MacroHFT: Memory Augmented Context-aware Reinforcement Learning On High Frequency Trading

problem: Diversity-driven RL ensembles can generalize to dynamics shifts, but unconstrained diversity yields unsafe or impractical behaviors and too few feasible policies for real deployments.
method: Diversity in Regulation (DiR): maximize disagreement between per-policy inverse dynamics models conditioned on filtered partial state pairs (f(s), f(s')). A customizable state filtration function f removes chosen state variables so only targeted behavior dimensions (e.g., leg motions) are diversified, yielding regulated, feasible behavior patterns. Implement with an IDM per policy trained by MLE and use the disagreement as an intrinsic reward to guide policy learning.
paper_title: Open-Ended Diverse Solution Discovery with Regulated Behavior Patterns for Cross-Domain Adaptation

problem: Prior methods train fixed-size populations with pairwise constraints, causing heavy computation, non-stationary training dynamics, and limited coverage of behavior space.
method: Open-ended, single-policy iterative discovery: train one policy at a time to differ from previously discovered policies (frozen IDMs) via a Lagrangian-relaxed objective. This avoids pairwise online constraints, scales to arbitrarily many policies, and provides more stable, stationary intrinsic rewards than simultaneously trained discriminators.
paper_title: Open-Ended Diverse Solution Discovery with Regulated Behavior Patterns for Cross-Domain Adaptation

problem: Designing a tractable diversity objective that encourages distinct behaviors without collapsing policy entropy is challenging.
method: Convert inverse dynamics divergence into a cross-entropy intrinsic reward between current transitions and prior policies' IDMs under f(s), f(s'), yielding a lower-entropy-penalizing objective. This objective is theoretically tied to conditional mutual information I(a; z | f(s), f(s')) and is optimized with PPO, enabling stable, scalable discovery of diverse yet controlled behaviors.
paper_title: Open-Ended Diverse Solution Discovery with Regulated Behavior Patterns for Cross-Domain Adaptation

problem: Existing sample-complexity analyses for learning Nash equilibria in mean-field games (MFGs) either rely on strong structural assumptions (e.g., contractivity/monotonicity) or use complexity measures (MBED) that can be exponentially large, obscuring whether MFGs are fundamentally harder than single-agent RL.
method: Introduce Partial Model-Based Eluder Dimension (P-MBED), which fixes the density input via an adversarially chosen reference policy and measures the eluder dimension of the induced single-agent model class. Prove P-MBED can be dramatically smaller than MBED (e.g., tabular P-MBED ≤ |S||A|; linear MFGs ˜O(d_sa)), thus tightly connecting MFG learning difficulty to single-agent complexity.
paper_title: Model-Based RL for Mean-Field Games is not Statistically Harder than Single-Agent RL

problem: Design a general, sample-efficient exploration algorithm to learn NE in MFGs under only realizability and Lipschitz continuity, without contractivity/monotonicity, and clarify the statistical relation to single-agent RL.
method: Propose a model-elimination framework (MEBP) that repeatedly: (i) runs a single-agent model-elimination subroutine conditioned on a reference policy, and (ii) uses a novel bridge policy constructed via a Policy-Aware Model (PAM) and interpolation over central models to ensure either halving the hypothesis class or outputting an ε-NE. Establish sample complexity polynomial in P-MBED and logarithmic in |M|, showing that learning MFG NE is no harder than solving O(log|M|) single-agent RL problems; additionally prove an exponential separation showing MFC can be statistically harder.
paper_title: Model-Based RL for Mean-Field Games is not Statistically Harder than Single-Agent RL

problem: There is no general sample-complexity theory for learning NE in heterogeneous Multi-Type MFGs (MT-MFGs), which model multiple agent types with coupled dynamics/rewards.
method: Reduce MT-MFGs to a lifted MFG with a constrained policy space and define a Multi-Type P-MBED that sums per-type complexities. Adapt the model-elimination and bridge-policy machinery to the constrained setting, yielding the first sample-complexity bounds polynomial in the (sum of) per-type P-MBED and independent of the number of agents; also connect to Multi-Type Symmetric Anonymous Games via mean-field approximation guarantees.
paper_title: Model-Based RL for Mean-Field Games is not Statistically Harder than Single-Agent RL

problem: QWK aligns AES with human raters but is non-differentiable and typically computed batch-wise, yielding the same reward for all samples and causing unstable training; multi-trait AES also needs fine-grained per-trait accuracy.
method: Design a scoring-aware multi-reward RL objective (SaMRL) that combines a bidirectional QWK reward—batch-wise QWK over essays and trait-wise QWK within each sample—to capture rater agreement while stabilizing credit assignment, together with a mean trait-wise (R)MSE penalty to enforce precise score-level accuracy; optimize with PPO.
paper_title: Autoregressive Multi-trait Essay Scoring via Reinforcement Learning with Scoring-aware Multiple Rewards

problem: RL for AES has been confined to classification (to obtain action probabilities), which underperforms regression, especially with wide score ranges; regression lacks a probability distribution for policy gradients, blocking RL use.
method: Reformulate AES as autoregressive generation of [trait, score] tokens with a seq2seq (T5) model, using token-generation probabilities as the policy for policy-gradient updates; treat the full multi-trait sequence as an episode and apply rewards at sequence end, enabling direct optimization of QWK/MSE within a strong generation-based scorer.
paper_title: Autoregressive Multi-trait Essay Scoring via Reinforcement Learning with Scoring-aware Multiple Rewards

problem: Pure reward optimization can distort output structure and trait order in multi-trait generation, and fixed mixing of multiple rewards may misbalance objectives over training.
method: Stabilize policy learning by constraining the trainable policy to a frozen anchor model via token-wise KL regularization to preserve the [trait_j score_j] format and ordering; jointly learn dynamic weights for the QWK and MSE PPO losses (softmax-normalized) to adaptively balance objectives during training.
paper_title: Autoregressive Multi-trait Essay Scoring via Reinforcement Learning with Scoring-aware Multiple Rewards

problem: RL locomotion often needs tedious reward tuning for desired styles, and AMP cannot intentionally switch among multiple styles, risking hybrids or defaulting to the easiest style.
method: Multi-AMP: extend AMP to multiple, discretely switchable styles by adding a one-hot style selector to the policy and training per-style discriminators on state-transition descriptors (phi(s_t), phi(s_{t+1})) with per-style rollout buffers; combine discriminator-derived style rewards with task rewards, enabling automatic imitation and explicit style selection across heterogeneous motion datasets (RL controllers, trajectory optimization, reversed clips).
paper_title: Advanced Skills through Multiple Adversarial Motion Priors in Reinforcement Learning

problem: Some target skills lack motion data, yet must be co-trained with motion-prior skills without catastrophic forgetting or breaking the unified policy.
method: Treat each skill as a style; for data-free skills set the style reward to zero while keeping the one-hot style selector and shared policy within the Multi-AMP framework; train all tasks in parallel in a command-conditioned setup, achieving multi-skill learning comparable to specialized single-task policies.
paper_title: Advanced Skills through Multiple Adversarial Motion Priors in Reinforcement Learning

problem: Designing safe, low-impact sit-down motions is difficult due to conflicting task rewards and high impact on hardware, leading to extensive reward tuning.
method: Generate a sit-down motion prior by reversing a recorded stand-up trajectory and use it in Multi-AMP to provide a discriminator-driven style reward; temporarily suppress conflicting task rewards after sit-down command to avoid style breaking; yields gentle, feasible sit-down on a real wheeled-legged quadruped and enables switching between quadruped and humanoid configurations.
paper_title: Advanced Skills through Multiple Adversarial Motion Priors in Reinforcement Learning

problem: Model-free RL schedulers for heterogeneous clusters are sample-inefficient under partial observability and hidden resource contention (e.g., PCIe bottlenecks).
method: Introduce a domain-guided Bayesian-model-based POMDP: a Bayesian network encodes architectural invariants and performance-counter relationships to infer latent resource utilization as the belief state, which conditions A3C policy/value networks—an inductive bias that greatly reduces required training data.
paper_title: Inductive-bias-driven Reinforcement Learning for Efficient Schedules in Heterogeneous Clusters

problem: Training deep RL with a Bayesian belief model is slow because full probabilistic inference is required at each update and is NP-hard even approximately.
method: Develop a sampling-based gradient estimation for Bayesian networks that computes parameter gradients by generating samples and recursively exploiting BN structure (Bayes-ball for independences), avoiding full posterior inference—reducing training time versus belief propagation and black-box RL.
paper_title: Inductive-bias-driven Reinforcement Learning for Efficient Schedules in Heterogeneous Clusters

problem: Existing schedulers and black-box learners cannot jointly reason over hardware topology, workload dataflow, and temporal dynamics for CPUs/GPUs/FPGAs, leading to suboptimal scheduling and contention.
method: Symphony couples the BN-inferred belief with graph-network embeddings of the system topology and the workload DFG plus an LSTM policy head to choose execution/reconfiguration/No-Op actions, trained end-to-end with a makespan-based reward—achieving near-oracle performance and beating heuristic/black-box baselines.
paper_title: Inductive-bias-driven Reinforcement Learning for Efficient Schedules in Heterogeneous Clusters

problem: RL policies for legged manipulators typically achieve contact-rich behaviors implicitly and cannot explicitly track a desired end-effector force, limiting compliance and safety—often assuming force/torque sensing.
method: Train a single whole-body, force-command-conditioned RL policy (outputs joint PD targets) to directly track a 3D end-effector force across a large workspace without F/T sensors. In simulation, introduce a soft potential-field spring–damper to emulate contact and alternate between position- and force-tracking modes with dedicated rewards, enabling sim-to-real deployment on a quadruped with arm.
paper_title: Learning Force Control for Legged Manipulation

problem: Achieving accurate force regulation on real hardware without direct force feedback is difficult due to partial observability and sim-to-real gaps.
method: Concurrent state estimation: learn a network that predicts privileged quantities (gripper pose/velocity, external force) from proprioceptive history; feed the estimate to the actor while the critic uses true privileged state (learning-by-cheating setup). This enables robust force tracking and estimation on hardware using only joint encoders and IMU.
paper_title: Learning Force Control for Legged Manipulation

problem: Teleoperation of loco-manipulation requires variable compliance/impedance and intuitive control despite human–robot morphology mismatch.
method: Close the loop around the learned force controller to realize impedance control (F_cmd = -Kp(x - x_des) - Kd xdot) using the learned state estimates, and provide a teleop interface where the human commands end-effector motion/force while the policy coordinates whole-body posture and locomotion. This yields gravity compensation, compliant interaction, kinesthetic teaching, and strong whole-body force application across an expanded workspace.
paper_title: Learning Force Control for Legged Manipulation

problem: Reward models trained with Bradley-Terry (BT) loss over-optimize and fail to generalize to unseen prompts/responses due to over-confidence from inflated reward magnitudes.
method: Diagnose the root cause as excessive dispersion of hidden-state norms (while the projection head norm stays ~1); then add batch-wise sum-to-zero regularization (BSR) to the BT objective to enforce zero-centered rewards per batch. This softly penalizes extreme reward magnitudes via gradients proportional to r(x,y), curbing hidden-state norm variance and improving OOD robustness versus hinge, logit normalization, and margin-boosting baselines.
paper_title: On the Robustness of Reward Models for Language Model Alignment

problem: Evaluating RM robustness lacks a controlled, fine-grained view of distribution shift.
method: Define four generalization regimes by prompt/response disjointness (in-domain, prompt-disjoint, response-disjoint, mutual-disjoint) and use a fixed gold preference model (ArmoRM) to label pairs. Measure accuracy/Kendall’s tau to reveal brittleness to unseen response styles and show BSR consistently improves alignment across regimes.
paper_title: On the Robustness of Reward Models for Language Model Alignment

problem: Over-optimized RMs can misguide RLHF, leading to unstable training and verbosity/reward gaming.
method: Integrate BSR-trained RMs into RLOO and track gold-reward alignment and reward–KL trajectories; complement with effective-rank analysis of hidden states to quantify parametric overfitting. BSR yields stable reward growth aligned with the gold RM, 40% shorter outputs and +7% LC AlpacaEval win rate, and better RM-Bench hard accuracy at 8B scale.
paper_title: On the Robustness of Reward Models for Language Model Alignment

problem: LLM-augmented recommenders in Euclidean space struggle to capture the hierarchical structure inherent in textual/semantic data, limiting their ability to model user preferences.
method: Use LLMs to generate multi-level (three-tier) parent–child tags for each item; build a tag–tag and tag–item graph and jointly learn tag–item and user–item interactions entirely in hyperbolic space; align the two item views with hyperbolic contrastive learning, injecting explicit structural hierarchies into the recommender.
paper_title: Large Language Models Enhanced Hyperbolic Space Recommender Systems

problem: Semantic embeddings from LLM-derived summaries are poorly aligned with collaborative signals and hyperbolic geometry, causing unstable training and weak fusion when naively combined.
method: A meta-optimized two-phase strategy with a Mixture-of-Experts adaptor: (1) generate user/item preference summaries via LLMs and encode them; freeze both the semantic encoder and the hyperbolic recommender, and train only the MoE to map semantic embeddings into the hyperbolic collaborative space using a hyperbolic margin ranking loss with a small task-specific margin to extract hierarchical semantics; (2) initialize ID embeddings with the transformed embeddings and train the hyperbolic model (with its own margin), bridging the semantic–collaborative gap, improving stability, and accelerating convergence.
paper_title: Large Language Models Enhanced Hyperbolic Space Recommender Systems

problem: Learned local feature descriptors are typically trained with pair/triplet surrogate losses that are misaligned with the downstream nearest-neighbor matching objective, requiring complex hard-negative heuristics and not directly optimizing ranking quality.
method: A listwise learning-to-rank formulation that directly optimizes Average Precision (AP) of nearest-neighbor retrieval using deep neural networks. Sorting is made differentiable via soft histogram binning; for binary descriptors use tanh-relaxed bits and tie-aware AP; for real-valued descriptors introduce a novel distance-quantization (histogram) approximation to enable end-to-end AP optimization. Works for both binary and real-valued descriptors.
paper_title: Local Descriptors Optimized for Average Precision

problem: Geometric noise (e.g., rotation, affine distortions) in patches degrades descriptor matching, and naive spatial transformations cause boundary artifacts.
method: Embed a Spatial Transformer module that predicts a 6-DOF affine alignment within the descriptor network, coupled with boundary-replication padding to avoid out-of-bounds artifacts, improving invariance without extra supervision.
paper_title: Local Descriptors Optimized for Average Precision

problem: In HPatches image matching, distractors are in-sequence and harder than out-of-sequence negatives; naive label mining risks errors due to repeating structures, limiting training effectiveness.
method: Clustering-based label mining within each sequence using HOG+pixel features to select in-sequence distractors via inter-cluster distance thresholds, combined with a minibatch sampler that packs two sequences per batch to expose harder negatives; improves image matching mAP.
paper_title: Local Descriptors Optimized for Average Precision

problem: Existing propensity estimators for unbiased learning-to-rank assume the Position-Based Model (PBM), making examination bias depend only on rank and not on query/user context, leading to mis-specified IPS weights.
method: Introduce the Contextual Position-Based Model (CPBM) that models examination propensity as Pr(E=1 | k, x) with a deep network h(k, x), allowing propensities to vary with arbitrary context features (query, user, candidate-set) while remaining compatible with IPS-based LTR.
paper_title: Intervention Harvesting for Context-Dependent Examination-Bias Estimation

problem: Estimating context-dependent propensities typically requires disruptive randomized swaps or learning full document-level relevance, which is hard and biases propensity estimates.
method: Propose intervention harvesting: reuse logs from multiple historic rankers (e.g., A/B tests or sequential deployments under stationary Pr(X)) to construct interventional sets S_{k,k′} where the same (x,d) appears at different ranks across rankers, and weight by assignment probabilities to control for relevance without explicit swaps.
paper_title: Intervention Harvesting for Context-Dependent Examination-Bias Estimation

problem: Even with harvested interventions, disentangling examination from (average) relevance across contexts and positions is challenging without a robust estimator.
method: Develop the AllPairs estimator that forms IPS-weighted labels across all position pairs and jointly learns a context-dependent propensity model h(k,x) and a simple average-relevance model g(k,k′,x) via a weighted cross-entropy objective; implemented with MLPs (including a symmetric relevance matrix), it avoids document-level relevance modeling and improves accuracy and robustness in real and semi-synthetic LTR settings.
paper_title: Intervention Harvesting for Context-Dependent Examination-Bias Estimation

problem: Graph-based collaborative filtering degrades under noisy and sparse user-item interactions, and existing graph contrastive learning relies on hand-crafted/random augmentations that can drop important signals, keep noise, and cause view collapse when both views share similar distributions.
method: AdaGCL introduces dual adaptive view generators: (i) a Variational Graph Auto-Encoder that samples structure-preserving graphs via KL-regularized latent variables to create diverse, generative views; and (ii) a learnable graph denoising generator that applies per-edge Bernoulli masks via Concrete reparameterization at each GNN layer to prune noisy links. The heterogeneous views are used with an InfoNCE objective on node self-discrimination, yielding informative and complementary supervision.
paper_title: Adaptive Graph Contrastive Learning for Recommendation

problem: Learned augmentations may be misaligned with the recommendation objective, limiting the utility of contrastive signals.
method: Task-aware optimization of view generators: augment the VGAE and denoising generators with BPR ranking loss computed from their embeddings, and jointly train with CF and SSL losses in a multi-task/bilevel manner. This steers generated views toward task-relevant patterns, stabilizes training, and mitigates collapse.
paper_title: Adaptive Graph Contrastive Learning for Recommendation

problem: Temporal modeling in recommendation is confounded by user behaviors that are irrelevant to the target item, which degrades learning of target-specific time-cycle patterns.
method: Introduce a gated filter unit that computes cosine similarity between the target item embedding and each past behavior’s item embedding and applies a thresholded gate to zero out irrelevant behaviors, retaining only target-related histories for temporal modeling.
paper_title: Denoising Time Cycle Modeling for Recommendation

problem: Discrete semantic time features (hour/weekday/month) break continuity, hurting modeling of absolute time cycles (ATC).
method: Absolute Time Convolution: aggregate surrounding semantic time slots with a 1D CNN and max-pooling across multiple granularities to smooth discrete time embeddings and preserve local time continuity for ATC.
paper_title: Denoising Time Cycle Modeling for Recommendation

problem: Relative time effects (how intent evolves with time since last interaction) are hard to encode so that similarity reflects time intervals and periodicity.
method: Bochner-based translation-invariant kernel embedding Φ(t) using trainable sinusoidal features so that inner products encode |t1−t2| similarity; combined with ATC in a time-cycle attention module to learn diverse periodic patterns for CTR prediction.
paper_title: Denoising Time Cycle Modeling for Recommendation

problem: Sparse retrieval-based speculative decoding relies on exact string matching over short contexts, leading to low precision/recall, low acceptance rates, and modest speedups.
method: DReSD replaces string matching with semantic dense retrieval: compute the LLM’s last-layer contextual token embedding of the current context, query a key–value datastore via approximate nearest neighbor search (ScaNN) keyed by embeddings to retrieve next-N token sequences, and filter candidates to start with the LLM’s own next token before exact, batched verification—yielding higher acceptance and longer accepted spans.
paper_title: DReSD: Dense Retrieval for Speculative Decoding

problem: High-dimensional LLM hidden states make dense retrieval slow and memory-heavy, hurting ANN efficiency and scalability for SD.
method: Introduce a dual-normalization + PCA pipeline for retrieval keys: z-score standardize embeddings (using ~1M sampled stats), reduce to ~64 principal components, then magnitude-normalize for cosine search. This compresses keys by ~30–40x while preserving retrieval quality (high MRR), enabling fast ANN; reuse the last-accepted-token embedding to seed the next iteration without extra compute.
paper_title: DReSD: Dense Retrieval for Speculative Decoding

problem: Dense SD effectiveness is highly sensitive to datastore/task mismatch and draft configuration, which can negate potential speedups.
method: Provide an empirical recipe: enforce datastore alignment (prompt domain, in-distribution responses from the target LLM, and matched sampling hyperparameters) and optimize draft shape via ablations—favor narrow-and-deep drafts (~3×20) for greedy decoding and more-but-shorter (~10×10) for nucleus—achieving up to 4.64× speedup over autoregressive decoding and large gains over sparse REST.
paper_title: DReSD: Dense Retrieval for Speculative Decoding

problem: CF lacks an explicit characterization of what constitutes high-quality user/item representations and what BPR actually optimizes.
method: Formalize alignment and uniformity on the unit hypersphere for CF and prove that perfectly aligned and uniform encoders are exact minimizers of the BPR loss with cosine similarity. Quantify these metrics and empirically trace training trajectories of representative CF methods (BPRMF, BPR-DS, LGCN, ENMF), showing that improving either property correlates with better recommendation performance.
paper_title: Towards Representation Alignment and Uniformity in Collaborative Filtering

problem: Existing CF objectives optimize alignment and uniformity only indirectly and often require negative sampling, making it hard to balance the two properties.
method: DirectAU: a negative-sample-free loss that directly minimizes squared L2 distance for positive user–item pairs (alignment) and in-batch uniformity losses for users and items separately via the log average Gaussian potential over pairwise distances of L2-normalized embeddings, with a single trade-off parameter. Using even a simple MF encoder, DirectAU consistently boosts top-K metrics and also integrates well with GNN encoders.
paper_title: Towards Representation Alignment and Uniformity in Collaborative Filtering

problem: Measuring global uniformity consistent with user/item distributions is computationally expensive when done over all interactions.
method: Propose an equivalent popularity-weighted estimator that samples users/items proportional to interaction counts to compute uniformity, reducing complexity while matching the true p_user/p_item and focusing the metric on dense regions.
paper_title: Towards Representation Alignment and Uniformity in Collaborative Filtering

problem: Listwise LLM rerankers are slow and inefficient because they generate full sequences of passage IDs to produce a ranking.
method: FIRST ranks candidates using only the output logits of the first generated identifier (single-token decoding). It extracts logits for each candidate’s single-token identifier (alphabetic A–Z to avoid multi-token BPE) and sorts them to produce the full order, cutting inference latency by ~50% without sacrificing accuracy.
paper_title: FIRST: Faster Improved Listwise Reranking with Single Token Decoding

problem: Language modeling objectives for listwise reranking penalize all ranking errors equally, failing to prioritize highly relevant passages.
method: Train the reranker with a learning-to-rank objective focused on the first-token logits: a weighted RankNet loss with inverse mean-rank weighting to emphasize top ranks, combined with the LM loss. This joint objective better calibrates first-token logits to reflect listwise order and improves nDCG on BEIR.
paper_title: FIRST: Faster Improved Listwise Reranking with Single Token Decoding

problem: Using LLM listwise reranker outputs for relevance feedback is non-trivial because they provide orderings, not numeric scores; prior work relied on cross-encoder scores with KL-divergence.
method: Perform inference-time relevance feedback by optimizing the retriever’s query vector using the LLM reranker’s ranked list distilled via the weighted RankNet loss, yielding higher recall@100 than cross-encoder-based feedback, with further gains when combining both signals.
paper_title: FIRST: Faster Improved Listwise Reranking with Single Token Decoding

problem: KG-enhanced recommenders are hurt by noisy/irrelevant triplets and lack a task-grounded way to identify which KG connections truly explain (rationalize) collaborative interactions.
method: An attentive knowledge rationalization module assigns task-related rational scores to KG triplets via relation-aware attention (HGT-inspired, using e_r ⊙ e_t context), normalized per head and made globally comparable with degree-scaling and Gumbel noise; these scores are injected into a heterogeneous KG aggregator to weight neighbors and are learned directly from CF labels.
paper_title: Knowledge Graph Self-Supervised Rationalization for Recommendation

problem: Generative SSL on KGs often uses random masking, failing to explicitly highlight and learn from the most informative knowledge connections.
method: A rationale-aware masked autoencoder that masks top-k highest-scoring (most important) KG triplets and trains the model to reconstruct them with a relation-aware dot-product loss using embeddings aggregated from the masked KG, thereby forcing the model to recover and emphasize critical rationale edges.
paper_title: Knowledge Graph Self-Supervised Rationalization for Recommendation

problem: Cross-view alignment between KG and user–item interaction signals suffers from noisy edges and false negatives under random augmentations.
method: Rationale-aware contrastive learning: construct debiased views by dropping low-score KG triplets and sampling u–i edges for dropout based on items’ mean rationale scores; derive view-specific embeddings (LightGCN for CF, rationale-aware KG aggregator), project to a shared space, and optimize a modified InfoNCE with controlled negatives to align cross-view rational semantics while resisting noise.
paper_title: Knowledge Graph Self-Supervised Rationalization for Recommendation

problem: Online recommender/CTR models degrade under non-stationary distribution shifts, and gradient-based (incremental) updates adapt too slowly for large models at serving time.
method: ReLoop2 adds a fast, training-free error compensation loop to a standard (slow) base CTR model. An error memory stores recently observed high-error samples keyed by the model’s hidden representation; at inference it retrieves top‑k similar keys and computes an attention‑weighted error estimate from neighbors’ labels and base outputs, then compensates the base prediction with a tunable weight to achieve immediate test-time adaptation without backprop.
paper_title: ReLoop2: Building Self-Adaptive Recommendation Models via Responsive Error Compensation Loop

problem: Serving-time retrieval of similar samples must be ultra-fast and memory-efficient under high-velocity streams; building/maintaining ANN indices or storing raw samples is too heavy in latency and RAM.
method: A locality-sensitive hashing (SimHash/SRP) based sketch memory with K arrays and L-bit hashes maps streaming embeddings to 2^L buckets and maintains per-bucket aggregates (counts/sums of labels and base predictions).
